details.etymology: From Old High German 'apful', related to English 'apple', with shared Proto-Germanic roots.
examples: Ich esse einen Apfel zum Frühstück. (I eat an apple for breakfast.)
Der Apfel ist rot und süß. (The apple is red and sweet.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Apfelsaft (apple juice), Apfelkuchen (apple cake), and the proverb 'Der Apfel fällt nicht weit vom Stamm' (the apple doesn't fall far from the tree).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wazzar', cognate with English 'water', from Proto-Germanic *watōr.
examples: Kann ich ein Glas Wasser haben? (Can I have a glass of water?)
Das Wasser ist kalt. (The water is cold.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like Mineralwasser (mineral water), Wasserhahn (faucet), and Trinkwasser (drinking water).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'brōt', cognate with English 'bread', from Proto-Germanic *braudą.
examples: Ich kaufe Brot beim Bäcker. (I buy bread at the bakery.)
Das Brot ist frisch gebacken. (The bread is freshly baked.)
details.usage: Germany has over 3,000 bread varieties; compounds include Schwarzbrot (dark bread) and Brotzeit (snack time, literally 'bread time').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hūs', cognate with English 'house', from Proto-Germanic *hūsą.
examples: Wir wohnen in einem großen Haus. (We live in a big house.)
Das Haus hat einen Garten. (The house has a garden.)
details.usage: Extremely productive in compounds: Rathaus (town hall), Krankenhaus (hospital), Hausaufgabe (homework).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kazza', ultimately borrowed from Late Latin 'cattus'.
examples: Die Katze schläft auf dem Sofa. (The cat sleeps on the sofa.)
Unsere Katze heißt Mimi. (Our cat is called Mimi.)
details.usage: Used in idioms like 'die Katze im Sack kaufen' (to buy a pig in a poke) and 'Katz und Maus spielen' (to play cat and mouse).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hunt', cognate with English 'hound', from Proto-Germanic *hundaz.
examples: Der Hund spielt im Park. (The dog plays in the park.)
Ich gehe mit dem Hund spazieren. (I go for a walk with the dog.)
details.usage: Appears in expressions like 'Hundewetter' (terrible weather) and 'Da liegt der Hund begraben' (that's the crux of the matter).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'miluh', cognate with English 'milk', from Proto-Germanic *meluks.
examples: Ich trinke jeden Morgen Milch. (I drink milk every morning.)
Die Milch ist im Kühlschrank. (The milk is in the fridge.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Milchkaffee (coffee with milk), Milchstraße (Milky Way), and Buttermilch (buttermilk).

details.etymology: From Latin 'schola', which came from Greek 'scholē' meaning 'leisure' or 'place of learning'.
examples: Die Kinder gehen in die Schule. (The children go to school.)
Die Schule beginnt um acht Uhr. (School starts at eight o'clock.)
details.usage: Forms compounds like Grundschule (primary school), Schuljahr (school year), and Fahrschule (driving school).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'buoh', related to 'Buche' (beech tree), as early Germanic runes were carved on beech wood.
examples: Ich lese ein Buch. (I am reading a book.)
Das Buch ist sehr interessant. (The book is very interesting.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Bücherei (library), Wörterbuch (dictionary), and Tagebuch (diary).

details.etymology: Shortened from 'Automobil', borrowed from French 'automobile', combining Greek 'autos' (self) and Latin 'mobilis' (movable).
examples: Mein Auto ist blau. (My car is blue.)
Wir fahren mit dem Auto zur Arbeit. (We drive to work by car.)
details.usage: Germany's car culture is central; compounds include Autobahn (highway), Autoschlüssel (car key), and Autofahrer (driver).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'frouwa' meaning 'lady, mistress', related to Proto-Germanic *frawjō.
examples: Die Frau liest eine Zeitung. (The woman reads a newspaper.)
Frau Müller ist meine Lehrerin. (Mrs. Müller is my teacher.)
details.usage: Used both as 'woman/wife' and as the formal title of address (equivalent to 'Ms./Mrs.'), replacing the now-outdated 'Fräulein'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'man', cognate with English 'man', from Proto-Germanic *mann-.
examples: Der Mann arbeitet im Büro. (The man works in the office.)
Mein Mann kocht heute Abend. (My husband is cooking tonight.)
details.usage: Means both 'man' and 'husband'; found in compounds like Ehemann (husband), Feuerwehrmann (firefighter), and Kaufmann (merchant).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kind', cognate with English 'kin', from Proto-Germanic *kinþą meaning 'offspring'.
examples: Das Kind spielt im Garten. (The child plays in the garden.)
Wie viele Kinder hast du? (How many children do you have?)
details.usage: Common in compounds: Kindergarten (nursery), Kindheit (childhood), Einzelkind (only child).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tag', cognate with English 'day', from Proto-Germanic *dagaz.
examples: Heute ist ein schöner Tag. (Today is a beautiful day.)
Guten Tag! (Good day!/Hello!)
details.usage: 'Guten Tag' is the standard daytime greeting; compounds include Geburtstag (birthday), Feiertag (holiday), and Alltag (everyday life).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'naht', cognate with English 'night', from Proto-Indo-European *nókʷts.
examples: Gute Nacht! (Good night!)
Die Nacht war sehr kalt. (The night was very cold.)
details.usage: Used in greetings (Gute Nacht) and compounds like Mitternacht (midnight), Nachtisch (dessert), and Nachtschicht (night shift).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stat' meaning 'place, site', related to 'stehen' (to stand), cognate with English 'stead'.
examples: Berlin ist eine große Stadt. (Berlin is a big city.)
Ich wohne in der Stadt. (I live in the city.)
details.usage: Found in compounds like Hauptstadt (capital city), Altstadt (old town), and Stadtmitte (city center).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'friunt', originally a present participle of 'friōn' (to love), cognate with English 'friend'.
examples: Er ist mein bester Freund. (He is my best friend.)
Ich treffe meine Freunde am Wochenende. (I meet my friends on the weekend.)
details.usage: Also means 'boyfriend' in context; the female form is Freundin (female friend/girlfriend).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tisc', borrowed from Latin 'discus' (disk, plate), which came from Greek 'diskos'.
examples: Das Essen steht auf dem Tisch. (The food is on the table.)
Wir sitzen am Tisch. (We sit at the table.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Schreibtisch (desk), Tischtennis (table tennis), and Nachttisch (nightstand).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stuol', cognate with English 'stool', from Proto-Germanic *stōlaz.
examples: Bitte setz dich auf den Stuhl. (Please sit on the chair.)
Der Stuhl ist aus Holz. (The chair is made of wood.)
details.usage: Compounds include Rollstuhl (wheelchair), Lehrstuhl (professorial chair/academic chair), and Hochstuhl (high chair).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'turi', cognate with English 'door', from Proto-Germanic *durz.
examples: Bitte schließ die Tür. (Please close the door.)
Die Tür ist offen. (The door is open.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Haustür (front door), Türklingel (doorbell), and the expression 'vor der Tür stehen' (to be imminent).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gelt' meaning 'payment, reward', related to 'gelten' (to be worth), cognate with English 'yield'.
examples: Ich habe kein Geld dabei. (I have no money on me.)
Geld allein macht nicht glücklich. (Money alone doesn't make you happy.)
details.usage: Very productive in compounds: Taschengeld (pocket money), Bargeld (cash), Geldautomat (ATM).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Turkish 'kahve', which came from Arabic 'qahwa', entering German in the 17th century.
examples: Ich trinke morgens Kaffee. (I drink coffee in the morning.)
Möchten Sie einen Kaffee? (Would you like a coffee?)
details.usage: Germany is one of Europe's top coffee consumers; 'Kaffeeklatsch' (coffee gossip) and 'Kaffeefahrt' (promotional trip) are cultural terms.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'riso', which came from Latin 'oryza', ultimately from Greek 'óryza' of Asian origin.
examples: Ich koche Reis zum Abendessen. (I cook rice for dinner.)
Der Reis ist noch nicht fertig. (The rice is not ready yet.)
details.usage: Typically uncountable in everyday use; compounds include Reisfeld (rice field) and Milchreis (rice pudding).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fisc', cognate with English 'fish', from Proto-Germanic *fiskaz.
examples: Freitags essen wir Fisch. (On Fridays we eat fish.)
Der Fisch schwimmt im See. (The fish swims in the lake.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Fischmarkt (fish market), Goldfisch (goldfish), and in the idiom 'stumm wie ein Fisch' (silent as a fish).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'arzāt', borrowed from Late Latin 'archiater' (chief physician), from Greek 'archiatros'.
examples: Ich muss zum Arzt gehen. (I have to go to the doctor.)
Der Arzt hat mir Medikamente verschrieben. (The doctor prescribed me medication.)
details.usage: Female form is Ärztin; compounds include Zahnarzt (dentist), Tierarzt (veterinarian), and Hausarzt (family doctor).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'hora' (hour) via Middle Low German, originally meaning 'hour' before shifting to mean the timepiece itself.
examples: Es ist drei Uhr. (It is three o'clock.)
Meine Uhr ist kaputt. (My watch is broken.)
details.usage: Means both 'clock/watch' and 'o'clock'; compounds include Armbanduhr (wristwatch) and Wanduhr (wall clock).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'jār', cognate with English 'year', from Proto-Germanic *jērą.
examples: Ich bin 25 Jahre alt. (I am 25 years old.)
Letztes Jahr war ich in Berlin. (Last year I was in Berlin.)
details.usage: Common in compounds: Neujahr (New Year), Jahreszeit (season), Jahrhundert (century), Schuljahr (school year).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'arbeit' meaning 'hardship, toil', from Proto-Germanic *arbaidiz, originally denoting hard physical labor.
examples: Ich gehe jeden Tag zur Arbeit. (I go to work every day.)
Die Arbeit macht mir Spaß. (I enjoy the work.)
details.usage: Central cultural concept; compounds include Hausarbeit (housework), Arbeitsplatz (workplace), and Zusammenarbeit (cooperation).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ezzan', cognate with English 'eat', used as a nominalized infinitive meaning 'food' or 'meal'.
examples: Das Essen schmeckt gut. (The food tastes good.)
Wann ist das Essen fertig? (When is the food ready?)
details.usage: Functions as both verb (essen = to eat) and noun (das Essen = food/meal); also the name of a major city in the Ruhr area.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zimbar' meaning 'timber, building material', related to the idea of a wooden room or structure.
examples: Das Zimmer ist sehr groß. (The room is very large.)
Ich suche ein Zimmer in einer WG. (I'm looking for a room in a shared apartment.)
details.usage: Found in compounds like Schlafzimmer (bedroom), Badezimmer (bathroom), Wohnzimmer (living room), and Klassenzimmer (classroom).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'muoter', cognate with English 'mother', from Proto-Indo-European *méh₂tēr.
examples: Meine Mutter kocht sehr gut. (My mother cooks very well.)
Die Mutter liest dem Kind ein Buch vor. (The mother reads a book to the child.)
details.usage: Compounds include Großmutter (grandmother), Muttersprache (mother tongue), and Muttertag (Mother's Day).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fater', cognate with English 'father', from Proto-Indo-European *ph₂tḗr.
examples: Mein Vater arbeitet als Lehrer. (My father works as a teacher.)
Der Vater spielt mit den Kindern. (The father plays with the children.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Großvater (grandfather), Vaterland (fatherland), and Vaterschaft (paternity).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bruoder', cognate with English 'brother', from Proto-Indo-European *bʰréh₂tēr.
examples: Mein Bruder ist älter als ich. (My brother is older than me.)
Hast du einen Bruder? (Do you have a brother?)
details.usage: Also used figuratively for close male friends or religious brothers; compound Geschwister means 'siblings'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'swester', cognate with English 'sister', from Proto-Indo-European *swésōr.
examples: Meine Schwester wohnt in München. (My sister lives in Munich.)
Sie hat zwei Schwestern. (She has two sisters.)
details.usage: Also means 'nurse' (Krankenschwester); compounds include Zwillingsschwester (twin sister) and Stiefschwester (stepsister).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'familia' meaning 'household, servants', entering German in the 16th century.
examples: Meine Familie ist sehr groß. (My family is very large.)
Am Sonntag essen wir mit der Familie. (On Sunday we eat with the family.)
details.usage: Compounds include Familienname (family name/surname), Familienfest (family celebration), and Großfamilie (extended family).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sunu', cognate with English 'son', from Proto-Germanic *sunuz.
examples: Mein Sohn geht in die Schule. (My son goes to school.)
Sie hat zwei Söhne. (She has two sons.)
details.usage: Found in compounds like Schwiegersohn (son-in-law) and in surnames ending in -sohn (equivalent to English -son).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tohter', cognate with English 'daughter', from Proto-Indo-European *dʰugh₂tḗr.
examples: Ihre Tochter ist fünf Jahre alt. (Her daughter is five years old.)
Die Tochter hilft der Mutter. (The daughter helps the mother.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Schwiegertochter (daughter-in-law) and Tochtergesellschaft (subsidiary company).

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'baby' in the 19th century, which itself is of imitative origin.
examples: Das Baby schläft. (The baby is sleeping.)
Sie bekommen ein Baby. (They are having a baby.)
details.usage: Widely used in German despite being an English loanword; the traditional German word is Säugling.

details.etymology: Informal shortening of 'Großmutter' (grandmother), a childish/affectionate form that became standard colloquial usage.
examples: Meine Oma backt den besten Kuchen. (My grandma bakes the best cake.)
Wir besuchen Oma am Sonntag. (We visit grandma on Sunday.)
details.usage: The informal everyday term for grandmother; the formal word is Großmutter.

details.etymology: Informal shortening of 'Großvater' (grandfather), a childish/affectionate form parallel to Oma.
examples: Mein Opa erzählt gerne Geschichten. (My grandpa likes to tell stories.)
Opa geht jeden Tag spazieren. (Grandpa goes for a walk every day.)
details.usage: The informal everyday term for grandfather; the formal word is Großvater.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ēltiron', comparative form of 'alt' (old), literally meaning 'the older ones'.
examples: Meine Eltern wohnen in Hamburg. (My parents live in Hamburg.)
Die Eltern kommen zum Schulfest. (The parents come to the school festival.)
details.usage: A plurale tantum (no singular form); compounds include Elternzeit (parental leave) and Elternabend (parent-teacher evening).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'oncle', which came from Latin 'avunculus' (maternal uncle).
examples: Mein Onkel wohnt in Berlin. (My uncle lives in Berlin.)
Onkel Thomas kommt zu Besuch. (Uncle Thomas is coming to visit.)
details.usage: Replaced the older German word 'Oheim'; children sometimes use it as a general respectful address for older men.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'tante', which came from Latin 'amita' (paternal aunt) via a child-language form.
examples: Meine Tante hat drei Kinder. (My aunt has three children.)
Tante Maria bringt immer Geschenke mit. (Aunt Maria always brings gifts.)
details.usage: Also used in the compound Tante-Emma-Laden (mom-and-pop shop, literally 'Aunt Emma's store').

details.etymology: Borrowed via Spanish/Portuguese 'banana' from a West African language (likely Wolof), entering German in the 18th century.
examples: Ich esse jeden Tag eine Banane. (I eat a banana every day.)
Die Banane ist gelb und reif. (The banana is yellow and ripe.)
details.usage: The idiom 'jemanden auf die Banane bringen' means to annoy someone; 'Bananenrepublik' is used as in English.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'orange', ultimately from Sanskrit 'nāranga' via Persian and Arabic.
examples: Ich hätte gerne eine Orange. (I would like an orange.)
Orangensaft ist mein Lieblingsgetränk. (Orange juice is my favorite drink.)
details.usage: In southern Germany and Austria, the word Apfelsine (from Dutch 'appelsien' = Chinese apple) is also used.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Spanish 'tomate', which came from Nahuatl (Aztec) 'tomatl'.
examples: Die Tomate ist rot. (The tomato is red.)
Ich brauche Tomaten für den Salat. (I need tomatoes for the salad.)
details.usage: In Austria also called Paradeiser; the idiom 'Tomaten auf den Augen haben' means to be oblivious.

details.etymology: From Italian 'tartufolo' (truffle), because the potato was initially compared to truffles as an underground tuber.
examples: Kartoffeln sind ein wichtiges Grundnahrungsmittel. (Potatoes are an important staple food.)
Ich schäle die Kartoffeln. (I peel the potatoes.)
details.usage: Central to German cuisine; compounds include Kartoffelsalat (potato salad), Bratkartoffeln (fried potatoes), and Kartoffelpuffer (potato pancakes).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'insalata' (salted), ultimately from Latin 'sal' (salt).
examples: Ich bestelle einen Salat. (I order a salad.)
Der Salat ist frisch. (The salad is fresh.)
details.usage: Refers to both lettuce and the prepared dish; the expression 'Da haben wir den Salat' means 'now we're in a mess'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'suppa', related to 'saufen' (to drink), cognate with English 'sup' and 'soup'.
examples: Die Suppe ist heiß. (The soup is hot.)
Ich esse gerne Tomatensuppe. (I like to eat tomato soup.)
details.usage: The idiom 'jemandem die Suppe versalzen' means to spoil someone's plans (literally: to over-salt someone's soup).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kuocho', cognate with English 'cake', from Proto-Germanic *kōkô.
examples: Meine Mutter backt einen Kuchen. (My mother bakes a cake.)
Möchtest du ein Stück Kuchen? (Would you like a piece of cake?)
details.usage: 'Kaffee und Kuchen' (coffee and cake) around 3-4 PM is a cherished German tradition; types include Käsekuchen and Apfelkuchen.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ei', cognate with Latin 'ovum' and English 'egg' (via Old Norse), from Proto-Indo-European *h₂ōwyóm.
examples: Ich möchte ein Ei zum Frühstück. (I would like an egg for breakfast.)
Das Ei ist hart gekocht. (The egg is hard-boiled.)
details.usage: Compounds include Spiegelei (fried egg), Rührei (scrambled eggs), and Osterei (Easter egg).

details.etymology: From Latin 'butyrum', which came from Greek 'boutyron' (cow cheese), entering Old High German as 'butera'.
examples: Ich brauche Butter für das Brot. (I need butter for the bread.)
Die Butter ist im Kühlschrank. (The butter is in the fridge.)
details.usage: Interestingly, its grammatical gender varies by region: die Butter (standard), der Butter (southern), or das Butter (some dialects).

details.etymology: From Latin 'caseus', cognate with English 'cheese', borrowed into Germanic very early.
examples: Ich esse gerne Käse. (I like to eat cheese.)
Der Käse kommt aus der Schweiz. (The cheese comes from Switzerland.)
details.usage: Colloquially 'Käse' also means 'nonsense'; compounds include Käsekuchen (cheesecake) and Käsebrot (cheese sandwich).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fleisc', cognate with English 'flesh', from Proto-Germanic *flaiski.
examples: Ich esse kein Fleisch. (I don't eat meat.)
Das Fleisch muss noch kochen. (The meat still needs to cook.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Fleischerei (butcher shop), Rindfleisch (beef), and Schweinefleisch (pork).

details.etymology: From Italian 'zucchero', ultimately from Arabic 'sukkar', which came from Sanskrit 'śarkarā' (gravel, sugar).
examples: Nimmst du Zucker in den Kaffee? (Do you take sugar in your coffee?)
Zu viel Zucker ist ungesund. (Too much sugar is unhealthy.)
details.usage: Compounds include Zuckerwatte (cotton candy), Puderzucker (powdered sugar), and Würfelzucker (sugar cube).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'salz', cognate with English 'salt', from Proto-Indo-European *séh₂ls.
examples: Kannst du mir das Salz geben? (Can you pass me the salt?)
Die Suppe braucht mehr Salz. (The soup needs more salt.)
details.usage: Historically valuable (Salzburg = 'salt castle'); compounds include Meersalz (sea salt) and Salzstreuer (salt shaker).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'obaz' meaning 'additional food eaten with bread', originally not specific to fruit.
examples: Obst ist gesund. (Fruit is healthy.)
Ich kaufe Obst auf dem Markt. (I buy fruit at the market.)
details.usage: A collective noun for fruit (individual fruits have their own words); compounds include Obstgarten (orchard) and Obstsalat (fruit salad).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'saf', cognate with English 'sap', from Proto-Germanic *sapą.
examples: Ich trinke gerne Orangensaft. (I like to drink orange juice.)
Der Saft ist frisch gepresst. (The juice is freshly squeezed.)
details.usage: Apfelschorle (apple juice mixed with sparkling water) is one of Germany's most popular drinks.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Dutch 'thee', which came from Hokkien Chinese 'tê', reflecting the maritime trade route.
examples: Möchtest du eine Tasse Tee? (Would you like a cup of tea?)
Ich trinke Tee mit Honig. (I drink tea with honey.)
details.usage: East Frisia (Ostfriesland) has Germany's strongest tea culture; compounds include Kräutertee (herbal tea) and Eistee (iced tea).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bior', possibly from Latin 'biber' (a drink), though the exact origin is debated.
examples: Ein Bier, bitte! (A beer, please!)
Deutschland ist bekannt für sein Bier. (Germany is known for its beer.)
details.usage: Germany's Reinheitsgebot (beer purity law) of 1516 is the world's oldest food regulation; Biergarten (beer garden) is a key cultural institution.

details.etymology: From Latin 'vinum', borrowed during Roman times when Romans brought viticulture to the Rhine region.
examples: Ich trinke gerne Rotwein. (I like to drink red wine.)
Der Wein kommt aus dem Rheingau. (The wine comes from the Rheingau.)
details.usage: Germany is known for white wines, especially Riesling; compounds include Weinberg (vineyard), Weinkarte (wine menu), and Glühwein (mulled wine).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'farawa' meaning 'appearance, color', related to Old English 'faru' (journey, appearance).
examples: Welche Farbe hat dein Auto? (What color is your car?)
Rot ist meine Lieblingsfarbe. (Red is my favorite color.)
details.usage: Means both 'color' and 'paint/dye'; compounds include Wasserfarbe (watercolor) and Haarfarbe (hair color).

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'rōt', cognate with English 'red', from Proto-Indo-European *h₁rewdʰ-.
examples: Rot ist die Farbe der Liebe. (Red is the color of love.)
Die Ampel zeigt Rot. (The traffic light shows red.)
details.usage: As a noun (das Rot) refers to the color itself; commonly used in compounds like Rotwein (red wine) and Rotkäppchen (Little Red Riding Hood).

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'blāo', cognate with English 'blue', from Proto-Germanic *blēwaz.
examples: Der Himmel ist blau. (The sky is blue.)
Blau ist eine beruhigende Farbe. (Blue is a calming color.)
details.usage: Colloquially 'blau sein' means 'to be drunk'; 'blaumachen' means to skip work/school.

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'gruoni', cognate with English 'green', related to 'grow' from Proto-Germanic *grōniz.
examples: Im Frühling wird alles grün. (In spring everything turns green.)
Grün steht für Hoffnung. (Green stands for hope.)
details.usage: Die Grünen is Germany's Green Party; 'ins Grüne fahren' means to go to the countryside.

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'gelo', cognate with English 'yellow', from Proto-Germanic *gelwaz.
examples: Die Sonne ist gelb. (The sun is yellow.)
Im Herbst werden die Blätter gelb. (In autumn the leaves turn yellow.)
details.usage: Associated with the postal service (Deutsche Post uses yellow); 'Gelbe Seiten' means 'Yellow Pages'.

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'swarz', cognate with English 'swarthy', from Proto-Germanic *swartaz.
examples: Er trägt einen schwarzen Anzug. (He wears a black suit.)
Schwarz ist eine elegante Farbe. (Black is an elegant color.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Schwarzbrot (dark rye bread), Schwarzwald (Black Forest), and 'schwarzfahren' (to ride without a ticket).

details.plural: — (used as a nominalized adjective, no standard plural)
details.etymology: From Old High German 'wīz', cognate with English 'white', from Proto-Germanic *hwītaz.
examples: Der Schnee ist weiß. (The snow is white.)
Sie trägt ein weißes Kleid. (She wears a white dress.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Weißwein (white wine), Weißbier (wheat beer), and Weißwurst (Bavarian white sausage).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fogal', cognate with English 'fowl', from Proto-Germanic *fuglaz.
examples: Der Vogel singt im Baum. (The bird sings in the tree.)
Im Park gibt es viele Vögel. (There are many birds in the park.)
details.usage: The expression 'einen Vogel haben' means to be crazy; compounds include Vogelhaus (birdhouse) and Zugvogel (migratory bird).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pfarifrit', shortened from Medieval Latin 'paraveredus' (post horse), of Celtic-Latin origin.
examples: Das Pferd läuft schnell. (The horse runs fast.)
Meine Tochter reitet gerne Pferd. (My daughter likes to ride horses.)
details.usage: Compounds include Pferdestärke (horsepower, abbreviated PS), Pferdeschwanz (ponytail), and Steckenpferd (hobby, literally 'stick horse').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kuo', cognate with English 'cow', from Proto-Indo-European *gʷṓws.
examples: Die Kuh steht auf der Weide. (The cow stands in the pasture.)
Kühe geben Milch. (Cows give milk.)
details.usage: The expression 'heilige Kuh' (sacred cow) is used as in English; 'Kuhhandel' means shady dealing.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mūs', cognate with English 'mouse', from Proto-Indo-European *muh₂s.
examples: Die Maus frisst Käse. (The mouse eats cheese.)
Die Katze jagt die Maus. (The cat chases the mouse.)
details.usage: Colloquially 'Mäuse' also means 'money'; 'Die Sendung mit der Maus' is a beloved German children's TV show.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bero', meaning 'the brown one', a taboo replacement to avoid speaking the animal's true name.
examples: Der Bär lebt im Wald. (The bear lives in the forest.)
Berlin hat einen Bären im Wappen. (Berlin has a bear in its coat of arms.)
details.usage: The bear is Berlin's heraldic symbol; 'jemandem einen Bären aufbinden' means to tell someone a tall tale.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tior', cognate with English 'deer' (which narrowed in meaning), from Proto-Germanic *deuzą.
examples: Welches Tier magst du am liebsten? (Which animal do you like most?)
Im Zoo gibt es viele Tiere. (There are many animals in the zoo.)
details.usage: Compounds include Tierarzt (veterinarian), Haustier (pet), Tiergarten (zoo/animal park), and Tierschutz (animal protection).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kopf' meaning 'cup, bowl', the meaning shifted to 'head' (replacing 'Haupt') due to the head's round shape.
examples: Mir tut der Kopf weh. (My head hurts.)
Er hat viele Ideen im Kopf. (He has many ideas in his head.)
details.usage: Common in idioms: 'Kopf hoch!' (chin up!), 'sich den Kopf zerbrechen' (to rack one's brains); compounds include Kopfschmerzen (headache).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ouga', cognate with English 'eye', from Proto-Indo-European *h₃ekʷ- (to see).
examples: Sie hat blaue Augen. (She has blue eyes.)
Das Auge sieht mehr als der Verstand. (The eye sees more than the mind.)
details.usage: Rich in idioms: 'unter vier Augen' (in private), 'ein Auge zudrücken' (to turn a blind eye); compound Augenarzt (eye doctor).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'nasa', cognate with English 'nose', from Proto-Indo-European *néh₂s.
examples: Meine Nase ist kalt. (My nose is cold.)
Er hat eine große Nase. (He has a big nose.)
details.usage: Many idioms: 'die Nase voll haben' (to be fed up), 'jemanden an der Nase herumführen' (to lead someone by the nose).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mund', cognate with English 'mouth', from Proto-Germanic *munþaz.
examples: Mach den Mund auf! (Open your mouth!)
Er nimmt kein Blatt vor den Mund. (He doesn't mince words.)
details.usage: Idioms include 'von Mund zu Mund' (by word of mouth) and 'Mundart' means 'dialect'; compound Mundwasser (mouthwash).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ōra', cognate with English 'ear', from Proto-Indo-European *h₂ṓws.
examples: Das Kind hat Ohrenschmerzen. (The child has an earache.)
Ich bin ganz Ohr. (I'm all ears.)
details.usage: Idioms include 'jemandem einen Floh ins Ohr setzen' (to put an idea in someone's head); compounds: Ohrring (earring), Kopfhörer (headphones).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hant', cognate with English 'hand', from Proto-Germanic *handuz.
examples: Gib mir bitte die Hand. (Please give me your hand.)
Ich wasche mir die Hände. (I wash my hands.)
details.usage: Extremely common in idioms: 'Hand in Hand' (hand in hand), 'alle Hände voll zu tun haben' (to have one's hands full); compounds include Handschuh (glove, literally 'hand shoe').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fuoz', related to Latin 'pes' and English 'foot', all from Proto-Indo-European *ped-.
examples: Ich habe mir den Fuß verletzt. (I hurt my foot.)
Wir gehen zu Fuß zur Schule. (We walk to school on foot.)
details.usage: Commonly used in the expression 'zu Fuß gehen' (to go on foot) and in compounds like 'Fußball' (football/soccer) and 'Fußgänger' (pedestrian).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'aram', related to Latin 'armus' (shoulder/upper arm) and English 'arm', from Proto-Indo-European *h₂ermos.
examples: Er hat einen starken Arm. (He has a strong arm.)
Sie nimmt das Kind auf den Arm. (She picks up the child in her arms.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Armband' (bracelet) and 'Armbanduhr' (wristwatch), and in the idiom 'jemanden auf den Arm nehmen' (to pull someone's leg).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bein', originally meaning 'bone' (as it still does in some dialects), later shifting to mean 'leg' in standard German.
examples: Mein Bein tut weh. (My leg hurts.)
Der Hund hat vier Beine. (The dog has four legs.)
details.usage: Still means 'bone' in compounds like 'Elfenbein' (ivory, literally 'elephant bone') and 'Gebein' (bones/remains).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hār', cognate with English 'hair', from Proto-Germanic *hērą.
examples: Sie hat lange blonde Haare. (She has long blonde hair.)
Ich muss mir die Haare waschen. (I need to wash my hair.)
details.usage: Used in idioms like 'um ein Haar' (by a hair's breadth) and compounds like 'Haarschnitt' (haircut) and 'Haarfarbe' (hair color).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'herza', cognate with Latin 'cor' and English 'heart', from Proto-Indo-European *ḱerd-.
examples: Mein Herz schlägt schnell. (My heart is beating fast.)
Sie hat ein gutes Herz. (She has a good heart.)
details.usage: Extremely common in emotional expressions and compounds like 'herzlich' (cordial), 'Herzinfarkt' (heart attack), and 'Liebesherz' (love heart).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hrukki/ruggi', related to English 'ridge', from Proto-Germanic *hrugjaz.
examples: Mein Rücken tut mir weh. (My back hurts.)
Er trägt den Rucksack auf dem Rücken. (He carries the backpack on his back.)
details.usage: Forms the basis of 'Rucksack' (backpack, literally 'back sack') and is used in idioms like 'hinter jemandes Rücken' (behind someone's back).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'būh', from Proto-Germanic *būkaz, originally meaning 'body' or 'trunk'.
examples: Ich habe Bauchschmerzen. (I have a stomachache.)
Der Bauch ist voll nach dem Essen. (The belly is full after the meal.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Bauchschmerzen' (stomachache), 'Bauchgefühl' (gut feeling), and 'Bierbauch' (beer belly).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fingar', cognate with English 'finger', from Proto-Germanic *fingraz, possibly related to 'fünf' (five).
examples: Er zeigt mit dem Finger auf die Karte. (He points his finger at the map.)
Ich habe mir in den Finger geschnitten. (I cut my finger.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Fingerabdruck' (fingerprint) and the idiom 'die Finger von etwas lassen' (to stay away from something).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zand/zan', cognate with English 'tooth' and Latin 'dens', from Proto-Indo-European *h₃dónts.
examples: Ich putze mir die Zähne. (I brush my teeth.)
Das Kind hat seinen ersten Zahn verloren. (The child lost its first tooth.)
details.usage: Found in many compounds like 'Zahnarzt' (dentist), 'Zahnbürste' (toothbrush), and 'Zahnpasta' (toothpaste).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hosa', originally meaning a leg covering or stocking, cognate with English 'hose'.
examples: Ich brauche eine neue Hose. (I need new pants.)
Die Hose ist zu lang. (The pants are too long.)
details.usage: In German, 'Hose' is singular (one pair of pants), unlike English; common in compounds like 'Jogginghose' (sweatpants) and 'Unterhose' (underpants).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hemidi', originally meaning an undergarment worn next to the skin, related to Proto-Germanic *hamiþją.
examples: Er trägt ein weißes Hemd. (He is wearing a white shirt.)
Das Hemd muss gebügelt werden. (The shirt needs to be ironed.)
details.usage: Typically refers to a button-down dress shirt; a T-shirt is 'T-Shirt', and compounds include 'Unterhemd' (undershirt) and 'Nachthemd' (nightgown).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kleid', from Proto-Germanic *klaiþą, meaning 'cloth' or 'garment'.
examples: Sie trägt ein rotes Kleid. (She is wearing a red dress.)
Das Kleid passt ihr sehr gut. (The dress fits her very well.)
details.usage: In singular, 'Kleid' means 'dress', but the plural 'Kleider' can mean 'clothes' in general, as in 'Kleiderschrank' (wardrobe).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scuoh', cognate with English 'shoe', from Proto-Germanic *skōhaz.
examples: Diese Schuhe sind sehr bequem. (These shoes are very comfortable.)
Zieh bitte deine Schuhe aus. (Please take off your shoes.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Sportschuhe' (athletic shoes), 'Handschuh' (glove, literally 'hand shoe'), and 'Schuhgeschäft' (shoe store).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Old French 'jaque' (a short coat), which derives from the name 'Jacques', used for a peasant's garment.
examples: Nimm deine Jacke mit, es ist kalt. (Take your jacket, it's cold.)
Die Jacke hat eine Kapuze. (The jacket has a hood.)
details.usage: Refers to various types of jackets; related compounds include 'Regenjacke' (rain jacket), 'Strickjacke' (cardigan), and 'Daunenjacke' (down jacket).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mantel', borrowed from Latin 'mantellum' (cloak), possibly of Celtic origin.
examples: Im Winter trage ich einen warmen Mantel. (In winter I wear a warm coat.)
Häng deinen Mantel an die Garderobe. (Hang your coat on the coat rack.)
details.usage: Refers to a longer overcoat as opposed to a 'Jacke'; used figuratively in 'den Mantel des Schweigens' (the cloak of silence).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'huot', related to the verb 'hüten' (to guard/protect), from Proto-Germanic *hōdaz, originally something that protects.
examples: Der alte Mann trägt einen Hut. (The old man wears a hat.)
Sie hat einen neuen Hut gekauft. (She bought a new hat.)
details.usage: Used in the idiom 'Hut ab!' (hats off!, expressing admiration) and 'das geht mir über den Hut' is a colloquial variant meaning something is too much.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'roc/hroc', originally meaning a tunic or upper garment, from Proto-Germanic *hrukkaz.
examples: Sie trägt einen kurzen Rock. (She is wearing a short skirt.)
Der Rock hat ein schönes Muster. (The skirt has a beautiful pattern.)
details.usage: In modern German, 'Rock' means 'skirt'; it is unrelated to English 'rock' (music), which is borrowed separately as 'Rockmusik'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tasca', likely borrowed from a Romance language, related to Italian 'tasca' (pocket/bag).
examples: Meine Tasche ist sehr schwer. (My bag is very heavy.)
Er hat die Hände in den Taschen. (He has his hands in his pockets.)
details.usage: Means both 'bag' and 'pocket'; compounds include 'Handtasche' (handbag), 'Hosentasche' (trouser pocket), and 'Taschenbuch' (paperback book).

details.etymology: Named after the semi-precious stone 'Beryll' (beryl), which was once used to make magnifying lenses in the Middle Ages.
examples: Ohne meine Brille kann ich nichts sehen. (Without my glasses I can't see anything.)
Er sucht seine Brille. (He is looking for his glasses.)
details.usage: Always singular in German for one pair of glasses (unlike English 'glasses'); compounds include 'Sonnenbrille' (sunglasses) and 'Lesebrille' (reading glasses).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sunna', cognate with English 'sun' and Latin 'sol', from Proto-Indo-European *sóh₂wl̥.
examples: Die Sonne scheint heute. (The sun is shining today.)
Wir sitzen in der Sonne. (We are sitting in the sun.)
details.usage: Prolific in compounds like 'Sonnenblume' (sunflower), 'Sonnenschein' (sunshine), 'Sonnenbrille' (sunglasses), and 'Sonnencreme' (sunscreen).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'regan', cognate with Latin 'rigare' (to wet/irrigate), from Proto-Germanic *regną.
examples: Es gibt heute viel Regen. (There is a lot of rain today.)
Wir gehen trotz des Regens spazieren. (We go for a walk despite the rain.)
details.usage: Very productive in compounds: 'Regenschirm' (umbrella), 'Regenmantel' (raincoat), 'Regenbogen' (rainbow), and 'Regenwurm' (earthworm).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'snēo', cognate with English 'snow' and Latin 'nix/nivis', from Proto-Indo-European *snóygʷʰos.
examples: Im Winter fällt viel Schnee. (A lot of snow falls in winter.)
Die Kinder spielen im Schnee. (The children are playing in the snow.)
details.usage: Rarely used in plural; common in compounds like 'Schneemann' (snowman), 'Schneeflocke' (snowflake), and 'Schneeball' (snowball).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wint', cognate with English 'wind' and Latin 'ventus', from Proto-Indo-European *h₂wéh₁ntos.
examples: Der Wind weht stark heute. (The wind is blowing strongly today.)
Ein kalter Wind kommt von Norden. (A cold wind is coming from the north.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Windmühle' (windmill), 'Windschutzscheibe' (windshield), and 'Windjacke' (windbreaker).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wolka', from Proto-Germanic *wulkō, possibly related to 'welk' (moist/damp).
examples: Dunkle Wolken ziehen auf. (Dark clouds are gathering.)
Am Himmel sind keine Wolken. (There are no clouds in the sky.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Wolkenkratzer' (skyscraper) and the expression 'auf Wolke sieben schweben' (to be on cloud nine).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'himil', from Proto-Germanic *hemilaz, possibly meaning 'cover' or 'canopy'.
examples: Der Himmel ist heute blau. (The sky is blue today.)
Im Himmel gibt es keinen Schmerz. (In heaven there is no pain.)
details.usage: Means both 'sky' and 'heaven'; used in exclamations like 'Um Himmels willen!' (For heaven's sake!) and compounds like 'Himmelfahrt' (Ascension Day).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wetar', cognate with English 'weather', from Proto-Germanic *wedrą, related to 'Wind' (wind).
examples: Wie ist das Wetter heute? (How is the weather today?)
Das Wetter wird morgen besser. (The weather will be better tomorrow.)
details.usage: One of the most common conversation starters in German; compounds include 'Wetterbericht' (weather report) and 'Wettervorhersage' (weather forecast).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kuhhina', borrowed from Latin 'coquina' (kitchen), derived from 'coquere' (to cook).
examples: Wir essen in der Küche. (We eat in the kitchen.)
Die Küche ist sehr modern. (The kitchen is very modern.)
details.usage: Also means 'cuisine' as in 'deutsche Küche' (German cuisine); compounds include 'Küchentisch' (kitchen table) and 'Einbauküche' (fitted kitchen).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bad', cognate with English 'bath', from Proto-Germanic *baþą.
examples: Das Bad ist neben dem Schlafzimmer. (The bathroom is next to the bedroom.)
Ich nehme ein heißes Bad. (I am taking a hot bath.)
details.usage: Means both 'bath' and 'bathroom'; also appears in spa town names like 'Baden-Baden' and in 'Schwimmbad' (swimming pool) and 'Badezimmer' (bathroom).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'betti', cognate with English 'bed', from Proto-Germanic *badją, originally meaning a sleeping place dug into the ground.
examples: Ich gehe früh ins Bett. (I go to bed early.)
Das Bett ist sehr bequem. (The bed is very comfortable.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Bettdecke' (blanket), 'Bettlaken' (bed sheet), and 'Etagenbett' (bunk bed), and in the phrase 'das Bett machen' (to make the bed).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fenstar', borrowed from Latin 'fenestra' (window), which may be of Etruscan origin.
examples: Kannst du bitte das Fenster öffnen? (Can you please open the window?)
Ich schaue aus dem Fenster. (I am looking out the window.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Fensterbrett' (windowsill), 'Schaufenster' (shop window), and 'Fensterplatz' (window seat).

details.etymology: From Latin 'lampas', via French 'lampe', ultimately from Greek 'lampas' (torch), derived from 'lampein' (to shine).
examples: Die Lampe im Wohnzimmer ist kaputt. (The lamp in the living room is broken.)
Mach bitte die Lampe an. (Please turn on the lamp.)
details.usage: Refers to any light fixture; compounds include 'Stehlampe' (floor lamp), 'Tischlampe' (desk lamp), and 'Straßenlampe' (street lamp).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'sofa', which came from Arabic 'ṣuffa' (a bench or raised platform covered with cushions).
examples: Wir sitzen auf dem Sofa und sehen fern. (We sit on the sofa and watch TV.)
Das Sofa ist sehr gemütlich. (The sofa is very cozy.)
details.usage: Interchangeable with 'Couch' in everyday German; 'Sofakissen' (sofa cushion) and 'Schlafsofa' (sofa bed) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'spiegal', borrowed from Latin 'speculum' (mirror), from 'specere' (to look).
examples: Sie schaut in den Spiegel. (She looks in the mirror.)
Der Spiegel an der Wand ist groß. (The mirror on the wall is large.)
details.usage: Also the name of Germany's leading news magazine 'Der Spiegel'; compounds include 'Spiegelei' (fried egg, because it looks like a mirror) and 'Spiegelbild' (reflection).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'trappa', related to Middle Low German 'trappe' (step), from Proto-Germanic *trappō (step/stair).
examples: Wir gehen die Treppe hinauf. (We go up the stairs.)
Die Treppe hat zwanzig Stufen. (The staircase has twenty steps.)
details.usage: Refers to a staircase or flight of stairs; compounds include 'Treppenhaus' (stairwell), 'Rolltreppe' (escalator), and 'Wendeltreppe' (spiral staircase).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'garto', cognate with English 'garden' and 'yard', from Proto-Germanic *gardô (enclosure).
examples: Die Kinder spielen im Garten. (The children are playing in the garden.)
Wir haben einen schönen Garten. (We have a beautiful garden.)
details.usage: Very common in compounds: 'Kindergarten' (kindergarten, literally 'children's garden'), 'Biergarten' (beer garden), and 'Schrebergarten' (allotment garden).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'want', from Proto-Germanic *wanduz, related to 'winden' (to wind/weave), originally referring to a woven partition.
examples: Das Bild hängt an der Wand. (The picture hangs on the wall.)
Die Wand ist weiß gestrichen. (The wall is painted white.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to an interior wall (as opposed to 'Mauer', an exterior or freestanding wall); used in 'Wandschrank' (wall cabinet) and 'Leinwand' (canvas/screen).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bodam', cognate with English 'bottom', from Proto-Germanic *budmaz.
examples: Das Buch liegt auf dem Boden. (The book is lying on the floor.)
Der Boden im Haus ist aus Holz. (The floor in the house is made of wood.)
details.usage: Means both 'floor' and 'ground/soil'; compounds include 'Fußboden' (floor), 'Dachboden' (attic), and 'Erdboden' (ground).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'dah', cognate with English 'thatch', from Proto-Germanic *þakam (covering/roof).
examples: Die Katze sitzt auf dem Dach. (The cat is sitting on the roof.)
Das Dach muss repariert werden. (The roof needs to be repaired.)
details.usage: Used in the idiom 'unter Dach und Fach' (all wrapped up/settled) and in compounds like 'Dachboden' (attic), 'Dachgeschoss' (top floor), and 'Dachziegel' (roof tile).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sluzzil', derived from 'sliozan' (to close/lock), cognate with the verb 'schließen' (to close).
examples: Ich habe meinen Schlüssel vergessen. (I forgot my key.)
Der Schlüssel passt nicht ins Schloss. (The key doesn't fit the lock.)
details.usage: Also used figuratively to mean 'key' to success; compounds include 'Schlüsselbund' (key ring), 'Schlüsselbein' (collarbone/clavicle), and 'Schlüsselwort' (keyword).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Old French 'tailloir' (cutting board), from Latin 'taliare' (to cut), entering German in the Middle Ages.
examples: Der Teller ist leer. (The plate is empty.)
Stell bitte die Teller auf den Tisch. (Please put the plates on the table.)
details.usage: Used in the expression 'über den Tellerrand schauen' (to look beyond one's own plate, meaning to think outside the box).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'glas', from Proto-Germanic *glasą, originally meaning 'amber' or a shiny substance, later applied to the transparent material.
examples: Kann ich ein Glas Wasser haben? (Can I have a glass of water?)
Das Glas ist zerbrochen. (The glass is broken.)
details.usage: Means both the material 'glass' and a drinking vessel; compounds include 'Weinglas' (wine glass), 'Trinkglas' (drinking glass), and 'Glasflasche' (glass bottle).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Old French 'tasse', which came from Arabic 'ṭāsa' (cup/goblet), ultimately from Persian 'tašt' (basin).
examples: Möchtest du eine Tasse Kaffee? (Would you like a cup of coffee?)
Die Tasse ist aus Porzellan. (The cup is made of porcelain.)
details.usage: Typically refers to a cup with a handle; used in the colloquial phrase 'nicht alle Tassen im Schrank haben' (to not have all cups in the cupboard, meaning to be crazy).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mezzisahs/mezzirahs', literally 'food knife', a compound of 'maz' (food) and 'sahs' (knife/blade).
examples: Das Messer ist sehr scharf. (The knife is very sharp.)
Gib mir bitte ein Messer. (Please give me a knife.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Taschenmesser' (pocket knife), 'Brotmesser' (bread knife), and in the idiom 'auf des Messers Schneide stehen' (to be on a knife's edge).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gabala', cognate with English 'gable' (originally a fork shape), from Proto-Germanic *gabalō (fork).
examples: Ich esse mit Messer und Gabel. (I eat with a knife and fork.)
Die Gabel liegt links neben dem Teller. (The fork is to the left of the plate.)
details.usage: Also used for other fork-shaped objects; compounds include 'Stimmgabel' (tuning fork) and 'Gabelstapler' (forklift).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'leffil', related to the verb 'laffan' (to lick/lap), from Proto-Germanic *lapōną.
examples: Ich brauche einen Löffel für die Suppe. (I need a spoon for the soup.)
Ein Löffel Zucker, bitte. (A spoonful of sugar, please.)
details.usage: Used in the colloquial expression 'den Löffel abgeben' (to give up the spoon, meaning to die) and in compounds like 'Teelöffel' (teaspoon) and 'Esslöffel' (tablespoon).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'topf', from Proto-Germanic *duppaz, meaning a deep vessel or pot.
examples: Das Wasser kocht im Topf. (The water is boiling in the pot.)
Stell den Topf auf den Herd. (Put the pot on the stove.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Kochtopf' (cooking pot), 'Blumentopf' (flower pot), and in the expression 'Topf und Deckel' (pot and lid, meaning a perfect match).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'strāza', borrowed from Latin 'strata (via)' meaning 'paved road', from 'sternere' (to spread/pave).
examples: Die Straße ist sehr lang. (The street is very long.)
Kinder spielen auf der Straße. (Children are playing on the street.)
details.usage: Used in addresses and compounds like 'Straßenbahn' (streetcar/tram), 'Autobahn' comes from a different root, but 'Hauptstraße' (main street) is very common.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'plaz', borrowed from Old French 'place', which came from Latin 'platea' (broad street), from Greek 'plateia'.
examples: Ist dieser Platz noch frei? (Is this seat still free?)
Wir treffen uns am Marktplatz. (We meet at the market square.)
details.usage: Means 'place', 'seat', or 'square'; common in compounds like 'Parkplatz' (parking space), 'Spielplatz' (playground), and 'Arbeitsplatz' (workplace).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'markat', borrowed from Latin 'mercātus' (trade/market), from 'mercari' (to trade).
examples: Am Samstag gehen wir auf den Markt. (On Saturday we go to the market.)
Der Markt hat frisches Obst. (The market has fresh fruit.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Supermarkt' (supermarket), 'Weihnachtsmarkt' (Christmas market), and 'Flohmarkt' (flea market).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'parc', which came from Medieval Latin 'parricus' (enclosed area), of Germanic origin.
examples: Wir gehen im Park spazieren. (We go for a walk in the park.)
Der Park hat einen großen See. (The park has a large lake.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Parkplatz' (parking lot/space), 'Freizeitpark' (amusement park), and 'Nationalpark' (national park).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kirihha', borrowed from Greek 'kyriakon' (house of the Lord), from 'kyrios' (lord).
examples: Die Kirche ist sehr alt. (The church is very old.)
Am Sonntag gehen wir in die Kirche. (On Sunday we go to church.)
details.usage: Refers to both the building and the institution; compounds include 'Kirchturm' (church tower), 'Kirchenglocke' (church bell), and 'Kirchenchor' (church choir).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bank' (bench), from Proto-Germanic *bankiz; the financial meaning developed because early Italian money-changers worked at benches ('banca').
examples: Wir sitzen auf einer Bank im Park. (We sit on a bench in the park.)
Ich muss zur Bank gehen. (I need to go to the bank.)
details.usage: Has two distinct plurals: 'Bänke' for benches and 'Banken' for financial banks; this is a classic example taught in German classes to illustrate how plurals can differentiate meanings.

details.etymology: From the verb 'laden' (to load/invite), originally referring to a counter or board on which goods were displayed for sale.
examples: Der Laden öffnet um neun Uhr. (The shop opens at nine o'clock.)
In diesem Laden gibt es gute Schokolade. (This shop has good chocolate.)
details.usage: A general word for 'shop/store'; compounds include 'Buchladen' (bookshop), 'Bäckerladen' (bakery shop), and 'Fensterladen' (window shutter, a different meaning).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'hôtel', from Old French 'hostel' (lodging), from Latin 'hospitale' (guest house), related to 'hospes' (guest/host).
examples: Wir übernachten in einem Hotel. (We are staying overnight in a hotel.)
Das Hotel hat ein Schwimmbad. (The hotel has a swimming pool.)
details.usage: Universally understood; related terms include 'Hotelzimmer' (hotel room), 'Hotelier' (hotelier), and the budget alternative 'Jugendherberge' (youth hostel).

details.etymology: Short for 'Kinematograph', borrowed from Greek 'kinema' (movement) and 'graphein' (to write), entering German in the early 20th century.
examples: Wollen wir ins Kino gehen? (Do we want to go to the cinema?)
Das Kino zeigt einen neuen Film. (The cinema is showing a new film.)
details.usage: The standard German word for cinema/movie theater; 'Kinokarte' (cinema ticket) and 'Kinofilm' (cinema film) are common compounds.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'museum', from Greek 'mouseion' (seat of the Muses), originally a place of study and the arts.
examples: Das Museum hat eine interessante Ausstellung. (The museum has an interesting exhibition.)
Am Sonntag besuchen wir ein Museum. (On Sunday we visit a museum.)
details.usage: Note the irregular plural 'Museen'; Berlin's 'Museumsinsel' (Museum Island) is a famous UNESCO World Heritage Site with five major museums.

details.etymology: A compound of 'krank' (sick) and 'Haus' (house), literally meaning 'house for the sick', formed in the German tradition of transparent compound words.
examples: Er liegt im Krankenhaus. (He is in the hospital.)
Das Krankenhaus ist in der Nähe. (The hospital is nearby.)
details.usage: The standard word for hospital; synonyms include 'Klinik' (clinic/hospital) and the more formal 'Spital' used in Austria and Switzerland.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'politia' (civil administration), from Greek 'politeia' (citizenship/government), entering German in the 15th century.
examples: Ruf die Polizei! (Call the police!)
Die Polizei hilft den Menschen. (The police help people.)
details.usage: Usually used without an article in phrases like 'Polizei rufen'; compounds include 'Polizist' (police officer), 'Polizeiauto' (police car), and 'Polizeiwache' (police station).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'apotheca', from Greek 'apotheke' (storehouse), from 'apotithenai' (to put away/store).
examples: Ich kaufe Medikamente in der Apotheke. (I buy medicine at the pharmacy.)
Die Apotheke ist um die Ecke. (The pharmacy is around the corner.)
details.usage: In Germany, pharmacies ('Apotheken') are strictly regulated and distinct from drugstores ('Drogerien'); they are marked by a red 'A' sign and are the only places to buy prescription medication.

details.etymology: A compound of the English loanword 'super' (from Latin 'super', meaning above/beyond) and 'Markt' (market), adopted in the mid-20th century.
examples: Ich gehe zum Supermarkt einkaufen. (I go shopping at the supermarket.)
Der Supermarkt hat bis 20 Uhr geöffnet. (The supermarket is open until 8 PM.)
details.usage: Major German supermarket chains include Aldi, Lidl, Edeka, and Rewe; 'Supermarkt' is sometimes shortened colloquially to 'Super' in some regions.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zug', derived from 'ziehen' (to pull/draw), originally meaning a pulling or drawing action, later applied to trains.
examples: Der Zug kommt um 10 Uhr. (The train arrives at 10 o'clock.)
Wir fahren mit dem Zug nach Berlin. (We travel by train to Berlin.)
details.usage: Has many meanings beyond 'train' including 'move' (in chess), 'trait', and 'draft' (of air); the German rail system 'Deutsche Bahn' is central to daily life.

details.etymology: Shortened from Latin 'omnibus' (for all), the dative plural of 'omnis' (all), first used for public transport vehicles in the 19th century.
examples: Der Bus kommt in fünf Minuten. (The bus comes in five minutes.)
Ich fahre jeden Tag mit dem Bus zur Arbeit. (I take the bus to work every day.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Busfahrer' (bus driver), 'Bushaltestelle' (bus stop), and 'Schulbus' (school bus).

details.etymology: A compound of 'fahren' (to drive/ride) and 'Rad' (wheel), literally 'driving wheel', coined in the 19th century as a German replacement for the loanword 'Bicycle'.
examples: Ich fahre mit dem Fahrrad zur Schule. (I ride my bicycle to school.)
Mein Fahrrad hat einen platten Reifen. (My bicycle has a flat tire.)
details.usage: Often shortened to 'Rad' in casual speech; Germany has extensive bike infrastructure, and compounds include 'Fahrradweg' (bike path) and 'Fahrradschloss' (bike lock).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Flug' (flight, from 'fliegen' - to fly) and 'Zeug' (thing/stuff), literally 'flying thing', modeled after 'Fahrzeug' (vehicle).
examples: Das Flugzeug fliegt nach New York. (The airplane flies to New York.)
Wir sehen ein Flugzeug am Himmel. (We see an airplane in the sky.)
details.usage: Part of a productive pattern: 'Fahrzeug' (vehicle), 'Werkzeug' (tool), 'Spielzeug' (toy) all use '-zeug' (thing/stuff) as a suffix.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'skif', cognate with English 'ship', from Proto-Germanic *skipą.
examples: Das Schiff fährt über den Ozean. (The ship sails across the ocean.)
Wir machen eine Schifffahrt auf dem Rhein. (We take a boat trip on the Rhine.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Schifffahrt' (shipping/boat trip, note the triple 'f' after spelling reform), 'Kreuzfahrtschiff' (cruise ship), and 'Raumschiff' (spaceship).

details.etymology: A compound of 'halten' (to stop/hold) and 'Stelle' (place/spot), literally 'stopping place', a transparent German compound.
examples: Die Haltestelle ist gleich um die Ecke. (The stop is right around the corner.)
Wir warten an der Haltestelle. (We wait at the stop.)
details.usage: Refers to bus and tram stops; for trains, 'Bahnhof' or 'Haltepunkt' is used instead; often abbreviated on signs and in speech as 'Bushaltestelle' or 'Straßenbahnhaltestelle'.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Bahn' (track/railway, from Old High German 'bana' meaning path) and 'Hof' (courtyard/yard).
examples: Der Bahnhof ist im Zentrum der Stadt. (The train station is in the center of the city.)
Wir treffen uns am Bahnhof. (We meet at the train station.)
details.usage: Used in the famous idiom 'Ich verstehe nur Bahnhof' (I only understand train station, meaning I don't understand anything); 'Hauptbahnhof' (main station) is abbreviated 'Hbf'.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Flug' (flight) and 'Hafen' (harbor/port), literally 'flight harbor', mirroring how airports function as ports for air travel.
examples: Der Flughafen ist weit von der Stadt. (The airport is far from the city.)
Wir müssen um sechs Uhr am Flughafen sein. (We have to be at the airport at six o'clock.)
details.usage: Major German airports include Frankfurt (FRA), Munich (MUC), and Berlin Brandenburg (BER); the compound follows the pattern of 'Hafen' being used for various types of ports.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Straße' (street) and 'Bahn' (track/railway), literally 'street railway', describing a tram or streetcar.
examples: Die Straßenbahn fährt alle zehn Minuten. (The tram runs every ten minutes.)
Ich nehme die Straßenbahn zur Arbeit. (I take the tram to work.)
details.usage: Also called 'Tram' in southern Germany and Austria; many German cities like Berlin, Munich, and Dresden have extensive tram networks.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stunta' (time/point in time), from Proto-Germanic *stundō, originally meaning a fixed point in time before narrowing to mean 'hour'.
examples: Die Fahrt dauert zwei Stunden. (The trip takes two hours.)
Ich habe eine Stunde gewartet. (I waited for one hour.)
details.usage: Also means 'lesson/class' as in 'Unterrichtsstunde'; compounds include 'Stundenplan' (timetable/schedule) and 'Stundenkilometer' (kilometers per hour).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'minuta' (small), short for 'pars minuta prima' (first small part), referring to the first division of an hour into 60 parts.
examples: Der Bus kommt in fünf Minuten. (The bus comes in five minutes.)
Warte eine Minute, bitte. (Wait a minute, please.)
details.usage: Used in everyday time expressions; 'Minutenzeiger' (minute hand) is a common compound, and 'auf die Minute genau' means 'to the exact minute'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wohha/wehha', cognate with English 'week', from Proto-Germanic *wikōn, possibly related to a sequence or change.
examples: Nächste Woche habe ich Urlaub. (Next week I have vacation.)
Ich gehe zweimal pro Woche schwimmen. (I go swimming twice a week.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Wochenende' (weekend), 'Wochentag' (weekday), and 'Wochenmarkt' (weekly market).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mānōd', related to 'Mond' (moon) and cognate with English 'month', as months were originally measured by lunar cycles.
examples: Ich bin seit drei Monaten in Deutschland. (I have been in Germany for three months.)
Welcher Monat ist jetzt? (Which month is it now?)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Monatsgehalt' (monthly salary), 'Monatsticket' (monthly pass), and 'monatlich' (monthly, as adjective/adverb).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mānetag', a calque of Latin 'Lunae dies' (day of the Moon), with 'Mond' (moon) + 'Tag' (day).
examples: Am Montag beginnt die Arbeitswoche. (On Monday the work week begins.)
Wir treffen uns nächsten Montag. (We meet next Monday.)
details.usage: German days of the week are all masculine (der); 'montags' (lowercase, with -s) means 'on Mondays' as a habitual action.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ziostag', named after the Germanic god Ziu/Tyr (god of war and law), parallel to English 'Tuesday' (Tyr's day).
examples: Am Dienstag habe ich einen Termin. (On Tuesday I have an appointment.)
Dienstags gehe ich zum Sport. (On Tuesdays I go to sports.)
details.usage: Like all German weekdays, it is used with 'am' for specific dates ('am Dienstag') and with a lowercase -s suffix for recurring events ('dienstags').

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mittawehha', literally meaning 'mid-week', a calque replacing the pagan god name (unlike English 'Wednesday' from Woden).
examples: Am Mittwoch ist die Woche halb vorbei. (On Wednesday the week is half over.)
Mittwochs haben wir frei. (On Wednesdays we are off.)
details.usage: Unlike most other Germanic languages that named this day after Odin/Woden, German chose the neutral 'mid-week' under Christian influence to avoid pagan references.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'frīatag', named after the Germanic goddess Frija/Frigg (goddess of love), parallel to English 'Friday'.
examples: Am Freitag gehen wir essen. (On Friday we go out to eat.)
Freitags ist das Restaurant voll. (On Fridays the restaurant is full.)
details.usage: Associated with the end of the work week; 'Freitagabend' (Friday evening) is a common compound, and 'Karfreitag' (Good Friday) is a public holiday in Germany.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sambaztag', borrowed from Greek 'sabbaton' via Latin, ultimately from Hebrew 'shabbat' (rest/Sabbath).
examples: Am Samstag gehen wir einkaufen. (On Saturday we go shopping.)
Samstags schlafen wir lange. (On Saturdays we sleep in.)
details.usage: In northern Germany, 'Sonnabend' (literally 'Sunday eve') is commonly used instead of 'Samstag'; both forms are standard German.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sunnūntag', a calque of Latin 'dies Solis' (day of the Sun), with 'Sonne' (sun) + 'Tag' (day).
examples: Am Sonntag sind die Geschäfte geschlossen. (On Sunday the shops are closed.)
Sonntags frühstücken wir zusammen. (On Sundays we have breakfast together.)
details.usage: In Germany, Sunday closing laws ('Sonntagsruhe') mean most shops are closed; 'Sonntagskind' (Sunday's child) refers to a lucky person.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'morgan', cognate with English 'morning/morrow', from Proto-Germanic *murganaz.
examples: Der Morgen ist kühl und frisch. (The morning is cool and fresh.)
Guten Morgen! Wie geht es dir? (Good morning! How are you?)
details.usage: As a noun means 'morning', but as an adverb 'morgen' (lowercase) means 'tomorrow'; 'Guten Morgen' is the standard morning greeting in German.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'aband', related to Old English 'aefen' (evening), of Germanic origin.
examples: Guten Abend, wie geht es Ihnen? (Good evening, how are you?)
Am Abend gehen wir ins Kino. (In the evening we go to the cinema.)
details.usage: Commonly used in the greeting 'Guten Abend' and in compounds like Abendessen (dinner) and Feierabend (end of the workday).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mittitag', a compound of 'mitti' (middle) and 'tag' (day), literally meaning 'middle of the day'.
examples: Um Mittag essen wir zusammen. (At noon we eat together.)
Heute Mittag scheint die Sonne. (This noon the sun is shining.)
details.usage: Frequently used in compounds like Mittagessen (lunch) and Mittagspause (lunch break).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zit', related to Old English 'tid' (time, tide), from Proto-Germanic *tidiz.
examples: Ich habe keine Zeit. (I have no time.)
Es ist Zeit zu gehen. (It is time to go.)
details.usage: One of the most common German nouns, appearing in compounds like Zeitschrift (magazine), Freizeit (free time), and Zeitplan (schedule).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'feriae' (holidays, days of rest), entering German in the 16th century.
examples: Die Ferien beginnen nächste Woche. (The holidays begin next week.)
In den Ferien fahren wir ans Meer. (During the holidays we go to the sea.)
details.usage: Used only in the plural form and typically refers to school or university holidays, unlike Urlaub which means vacation from work.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sumar', from Proto-Germanic *sumaraz, related to English 'summer'.
examples: Im Sommer ist es sehr warm. (In summer it is very warm.)
Diesen Sommer fahren wir nach Italien. (This summer we are going to Italy.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Sommerferien (summer holidays), Sommerzeit (summertime/daylight saving time), and Sommertag (summer day).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wintar', from Proto-Germanic *wintruz, possibly related to a root meaning 'wet' or 'water'.
examples: Im Winter schneit es oft. (In winter it often snows.)
Dieser Winter ist sehr kalt. (This winter is very cold.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Wintermantel (winter coat), Winterschlaf (hibernation), and Wintersport (winter sports).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'herbist', originally meaning 'harvest', cognate with English 'harvest' from Proto-Germanic *harbistaz.
examples: Im Herbst fallen die Blätter. (In autumn the leaves fall.)
Der Herbst ist meine Lieblingsjahreszeit. (Autumn is my favorite season.)
details.usage: Unlike English where 'harvest' shifted to mean crop gathering, in German Herbst retained its seasonal meaning and appears in compounds like Herbstferien (autumn holidays).

details.etymology: Derived from 'früh' (early) with the suffix '-ling', literally meaning 'the early one', referring to the early part of the year.
examples: Im Frühling blühen die Blumen. (In spring the flowers bloom.)
Der Frühling kommt bald. (Spring is coming soon.)
details.usage: Synonymous with 'Frühjahr' in German; commonly found in compounds like Frühlingsanfang (beginning of spring) and Frühlingsgefühle (spring fever).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'namo', from Proto-Germanic *namo, cognate with Latin 'nomen' and English 'name'.
examples: Wie ist Ihr Name? (What is your name?)
Ich kann mir seinen Namen nicht merken. (I cannot remember his name.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Vorname (first name), Nachname (surname), and Spitzname (nickname).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sprahha', derived from the verb 'sprechen' (to speak), from Proto-Germanic *sprekanan.
examples: Welche Sprachen sprechen Sie? (Which languages do you speak?)
Die deutsche Sprache ist interessant. (The German language is interesting.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Fremdsprache (foreign language), Muttersprache (mother tongue), and Sprachkurs (language course).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'diutisc', meaning 'of the people', derived from 'diot/thiot' (people, nation), from Proto-Germanic *theudiskaz.
examples: Ich lerne Deutsch. (I am learning German.)
Sein Deutsch ist sehr gut. (His German is very good.)
details.usage: As a noun (das Deutsch) it refers to the German language; it has no plural since it denotes a single language, and appears in compounds like Deutschkurs and Deutschlehrer.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'lehren' (to teach) with the agent suffix '-er', from Old High German 'lerari'.
examples: Der Lehrer erklärt die Grammatik. (The teacher explains the grammar.)
Mein Lehrer kommt aus Berlin. (My teacher comes from Berlin.)
details.usage: Refers to a male teacher; the female form is Lehrerin, and the profession is highly respected in German-speaking countries.

details.etymology: Formed from 'Lehrer' (teacher) with the feminine suffix '-in', following the standard German pattern for female agent nouns.
examples: Die Lehrerin ist sehr nett. (The teacher is very nice.)
Unsere Lehrerin gibt uns viele Hausaufgaben. (Our teacher gives us a lot of homework.)
details.usage: The standard feminine form of Lehrer; in modern German, gender-inclusive language debates often discuss alternatives like 'Lehrkraft' as a gender-neutral option.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scuolari', borrowed from Latin 'scholaris' (of a school), ultimately from Greek 'schole' (leisure, school).
examples: Die Schüler schreiben einen Test. (The students are writing a test.)
Er ist ein guter Schüler. (He is a good student.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to school pupils (not university students, who are called 'Studenten'), and forms compounds like Schülerausweis (student ID).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'cursus' (course, running), which also entered French as 'cours' before influencing the German form.
examples: Der Kurs beginnt um neun Uhr. (The course begins at nine o'clock.)
Ich besuche einen Deutschkurs. (I am attending a German course.)
details.usage: Used for educational courses and also for exchange rates (Wechselkurs) and stock prices (Aktienkurs).

details.etymology: Compound of 'auf' (up, on) and 'Gabe' (gift, giving), originally meaning 'something given up' or 'something assigned'.
examples: Die Aufgabe ist nicht schwer. (The task is not difficult.)
Hast du die Aufgaben gemacht? (Did you do the assignments?)
details.usage: Common in school contexts as Hausaufgabe (homework) and also means 'task' or 'duty' in professional settings.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fraga', from the verb 'fragen' (to ask), from Proto-Germanic *freganan.
examples: Ich habe eine Frage. (I have a question.)
Das ist eine gute Frage. (That is a good question.)
details.usage: Appears in many compounds like Fragebogen (questionnaire) and in the common expression 'keine Frage' (no question about it).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'antwurti', a compound of 'ant-' (against) and 'wurt' (word), literally meaning 'a word given back'.
examples: Die Antwort ist richtig. (The answer is correct.)
Ich warte auf deine Antwort. (I am waiting for your answer.)
details.usage: Pairs naturally with Frage (question) and is used in the verb form 'antworten' (to answer).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wort', from Proto-Germanic *wurdan, cognate with English 'word' and Latin 'verbum'.
examples: Ich kenne dieses Wort nicht. (I do not know this word.)
Das Wort hat viele Bedeutungen. (The word has many meanings.)
details.usage: Has two plural forms: 'Wörter' (individual, unconnected words) and 'Worte' (words in context, meaningful speech), and appears in compounds like Wörterbuch (dictionary).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'saz', derived from the verb 'setzen' (to set, place), originally meaning 'something set down'.
examples: Bitte lesen Sie den Satz vor. (Please read the sentence aloud.)
Der Satz ist zu lang. (The sentence is too long.)
details.usage: Means both 'sentence' in grammar and 'set' in sports (e.g., tennis), and appears in compounds like Satzbau (sentence structure).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stift' (pin, peg), from Proto-Germanic *stiftiz, originally referring to a pointed object.
examples: Hast du einen Stift für mich? (Do you have a pen for me?)
Der Stift schreibt nicht mehr. (The pen no longer writes.)
details.usage: A general term for any writing instrument; more specific forms include Kugelschreiber (ballpoint pen), Bleistift (pencil), and Buntstift (colored pencil).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hefti' (handle, that which is held), from the verb 'heften' (to fasten, stitch together).
examples: Schreib das bitte ins Heft. (Please write that in the notebook.)
Ich brauche ein neues Heft. (I need a new notebook.)
details.usage: Refers to a thin notebook or exercise book used in schools, and also means 'issue' of a magazine (Zeitschrift).

details.etymology: Borrowed via Latin 'papyrus' from Greek 'papyros', which referred to the Egyptian papyrus plant used for writing.
examples: Ich brauche ein Blatt Papier. (I need a sheet of paper.)
Hast du Papier und Stift? (Do you have paper and pen?)
details.usage: In the plural 'Papiere' it often means official documents or identity papers, and it appears in compounds like Toilettenpapier and Papierkram (paperwork).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'brevis (libellus)' meaning 'short (document)', via Old French, entering German in the early Middle Ages.
examples: Ich schreibe einen Brief an meine Mutter. (I am writing a letter to my mother.)
Der Brief ist gestern angekommen. (The letter arrived yesterday.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Briefkasten (mailbox), Briefmarke (postage stamp), and Briefträger (mail carrier).

details.etymology: Coined in the 19th century from Greek 'tele' (far) and 'phone' (voice, sound), adopted into German from the international scientific term.
examples: Das Telefon klingelt. (The telephone is ringing.)
Kann ich Ihr Telefon benutzen? (May I use your telephone?)
details.usage: Often shortened to colloquial forms, and used in compounds like Telefonnummer (phone number) and Telefonanruf (phone call).

details.etymology: A pseudo-anglicism coined in German in the 1990s, likely inspired by the English word 'handy' (convenient) but used as a noun to mean mobile phone.
examples: Wo ist mein Handy? (Where is my mobile phone?)
Bitte schalten Sie Ihr Handy aus. (Please turn off your mobile phone.)
details.usage: A classic example of a German pseudo-anglicism, as English speakers say 'mobile phone' or 'cell phone' rather than 'handy'.

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'computer' in the mid-20th century, which itself derives from Latin 'computare' (to calculate).
examples: Der Computer ist kaputt. (The computer is broken.)
Ich arbeite am Computer. (I work on the computer.)
details.usage: Fully integrated into German and used in many compounds like Computerspiel (computer game) and Computerprogramm (computer program).

details.etymology: Shortened from 'Fotografie', which comes from Greek 'phos' (light) and 'graphein' (to write), literally meaning 'writing with light'.
examples: Kann ich ein Foto machen? (Can I take a photo?)
Das ist ein schönes Foto. (That is a beautiful photo.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Fotoapparat (camera) and Fotoalbum (photo album); the spelling with 'F' instead of 'Ph' follows German orthographic reform preferences.

details.etymology: From Latin 'musica', from Greek 'mousike (techne)' meaning '(art) of the Muses', the Greek goddesses of arts and sciences.
examples: Ich höre gern Musik. (I like listening to music.)
Die Musik ist zu laut. (The music is too loud.)
details.usage: Has no plural form; used in compounds like Musikschule (music school), Volksmusik (folk music), and Musikinstrument (musical instrument).

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'film' in the early 20th century, which originally referred to a thin membrane, from Old English 'filmen'.
examples: Der Film war sehr gut. (The film was very good.)
Wir sehen heute Abend einen Film. (We are watching a film this evening.)
details.usage: Used for both cinema films and camera film rolls, and appears in compounds like Filmstar, Filmfestival, and Spielfilm (feature film).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'spil' (play, game, entertainment), from Proto-Germanic *spilam, of uncertain further origin.
examples: Das Spiel beginnt um drei Uhr. (The game begins at three o'clock.)
Kinder lieben dieses Spiel. (Children love this game.)
details.usage: Used broadly for games and matches, appearing in compounds like Spielplatz (playground), Brettspiel (board game), and Fußballspiel (soccer match).

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'sport' in the 19th century, which itself was shortened from 'disport' (amusement), from Old French 'desport'.
examples: Sport ist gesund. (Sport is healthy.)
Welchen Sport machst du? (What sport do you do?)
details.usage: Typically used without a plural (individual sports are called 'Sportarten'), and appears in compounds like Sportverein (sports club) and Sportplatz (sports field).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bal', from Proto-Germanic *balluz (ball), likely related to a root meaning 'to swell'.
examples: Wirf mir den Ball! (Throw me the ball!)
Die Kinder spielen mit dem Ball. (The children are playing with the ball.)
details.usage: Means both a sports ball and a formal dance event (as borrowed from French 'bal'), used in compounds like Fußball (football) and Basketball.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'reisa' (departure, journey), from Proto-Germanic *raisjan (to rise, set out).
examples: Die Reise war wunderbar. (The trip was wonderful.)
Wir planen eine Reise nach Spanien. (We are planning a trip to Spain.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Reisebüro (travel agency), Reisepass (passport), and Reiseführer (travel guide).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'urloup' meaning 'permission (to leave)', compound of 'ur-' (out) and 'loub' (permission).
examples: Ich bin im Urlaub. (I am on vacation.)
Wann hast du Urlaub? (When do you have vacation?)
details.usage: Refers specifically to vacation from work (unlike Ferien for school holidays), and appears in compounds like Urlaubstag (vacation day) and Urlaubsreise (vacation trip).

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'ticket', which came from Old French 'estiquet' (a note, label), related to 'etiquette'.
examples: Ich kaufe ein Ticket am Schalter. (I buy a ticket at the counter.)
Hast du das Ticket schon? (Do you already have the ticket?)
details.usage: Used alongside the native German word 'Fahrkarte' (for transport) and 'Eintrittskarte' (for admission); Ticket is more informal and increasingly common.
