details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'coffre' (chest, trunk), which came from Latin 'cophinus' (basket), ultimately from Greek 'kophinos'.
examples: Mein Koffer ist sehr schwer. (My suitcase is very heavy.)
Ich packe den Koffer für die Reise. (I am packing the suitcase for the trip.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Kofferraum (car trunk/boot) and Handkoffer (carry-on bag).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'lant', from Proto-Germanic *landan (land, territory), cognate with English 'land'.
examples: Deutschland ist ein schönes Land. (Germany is a beautiful country.)
Wir leben auf dem Land. (We live in the countryside.)
details.usage: Means both 'country' and 'countryside'; in Germany, the 16 federal states are called 'Bundesländer', and it forms compounds like Ausland (abroad) and Inland (domestic).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'meri', from Proto-Germanic *mari (sea, lake), cognate with Latin 'mare' and English 'mere'.
examples: Im Sommer fahren wir ans Meer. (In summer we go to the sea.)
Das Meer ist heute ruhig. (The sea is calm today.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Mittelmeer (Mediterranean Sea), Nordsee (North Sea uses 'See' instead), and Meeresfrüchte (seafood).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'berg', from Proto-Germanic *bergaz (mountain, height), related to the idea of a high or fortified place.
examples: Der Berg ist sehr hoch. (The mountain is very high.)
Wir wandern in den Bergen. (We are hiking in the mountains.)
details.usage: Appears in many place names (Heidelberg, Nürnberg, Salzburg) and compounds like Bergwerk (mine), Bergsteigen (mountaineering), and Eisberg (iceberg).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fluz', derived from the verb 'fließen' (to flow), from Proto-Germanic *fleutanan.
examples: Der Rhein ist ein langer Fluss. (The Rhine is a long river.)
Wir wohnen am Fluss. (We live by the river.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Flusstal (river valley) and Flussufer (riverbank); Germany's major rivers include the Rhein, Elbe, and Donau.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'seo', from Proto-Germanic *saiwiz (sea, lake); as masculine (der See) it means lake, while feminine (die See) means sea.
examples: Der See ist sehr tief. (The lake is very deep.)
Wir schwimmen im See. (We swim in the lake.)
details.usage: A notable example of grammatical gender changing meaning: 'der See' means lake, 'die See' means sea; appears in lake names like Bodensee and Chiemsee.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wald', from Proto-Germanic *walduz (forest), possibly related to 'wild'.
examples: Wir gehen im Wald spazieren. (We go for a walk in the forest.)
Der Wald ist sehr groß. (The forest is very large.)
details.usage: Forests are deeply significant in German culture and Romanticism; common compounds include Schwarzwald (Black Forest), Regenwald (rainforest), and Waldweg (forest path).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'boum', from Proto-Germanic *baumaz (tree), cognate with English 'beam' which originally also meant tree.
examples: Der Baum ist sehr alt. (The tree is very old.)
Im Garten steht ein großer Baum. (A big tree stands in the garden.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Weihnachtsbaum (Christmas tree), Apfelbaum (apple tree), and Baumhaus (treehouse).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bluoma', from Proto-Germanic *blomom (flower, blossom), related to 'blühen' (to bloom).
examples: Die Blumen sind sehr schön. (The flowers are very beautiful.)
Er schenkt ihr Blumen. (He gives her flowers.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Blumenladen (flower shop), Blumenstrauß (bouquet), and Sonnenblume (sunflower); giving flowers is a common social custom in Germany.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gras', from Proto-Germanic *grasam (grass), related to 'grün' (green) and the verb 'to grow'.
examples: Das Gras ist grün. (The grass is green.)
Die Kinder spielen auf dem Gras. (The children play on the grass.)
details.usage: The German proverb 'Das Gras wachsen hören' (to hear the grass grow) means to be overly perceptive or paranoid.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'erda', from Proto-Germanic *ertho (earth, ground), cognate with English 'earth'.
examples: Die Erde ist rund. (The earth is round.)
Die Blumen brauchen gute Erde. (The flowers need good soil.)
details.usage: Means both the planet Earth (capitalized) and soil/ground (lowercase), appearing in compounds like Erdbeere (strawberry, literally 'earth berry') and Erdbeben (earthquake).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'luft', from Proto-Germanic *luftuz (air, sky), of uncertain further origin.
examples: Die Luft ist frisch. (The air is fresh.)
Ich brauche frische Luft. (I need fresh air.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like Luftpost (airmail), Lufthansa (the German airline, literally 'air guild'), and Luftballon (balloon).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fiur', from Proto-Germanic *fuir, cognate with Greek 'pyr' (fire) and English 'fire'.
examples: Haben Sie Feuer? (Do you have a light?)
Das Feuer ist sehr heiß. (The fire is very hot.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Feuerwehr (fire department), Feuerwerk (fireworks), and Lagerfeuer (campfire).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sterno', from Proto-Germanic *sternon, cognate with Latin 'stella' and English 'star'.
examples: Die Sterne leuchten am Himmel. (The stars shine in the sky.)
Das Hotel hat fünf Sterne. (The hotel has five stars.)
details.usage: Used for celestial stars and rating systems alike; appears in compounds like Sternzeichen (zodiac sign) and is the symbol of the Mercedes-Benz brand.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mano', from Proto-Germanic *menon, related to the root for measuring time (as in English 'month' and 'moon').
examples: Der Mond scheint hell. (The moon shines brightly.)
Heute Nacht ist Vollmond. (Tonight there is a full moon.)
details.usage: Related to 'Monat' (month), and used in compounds like Mondlicht (moonlight), Vollmond (full moon), and Halbmond (half moon/crescent).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'wohnen' (to live, reside) with the noun suffix '-ung', from Old High German 'wonunga'.
examples: Meine Wohnung hat drei Zimmer. (My apartment has three rooms.)
Wir suchen eine neue Wohnung. (We are looking for a new apartment.)
details.usage: The most common word for apartment/flat in German; compounds include Wohnungssuche (apartment search) and Mietwohnung (rental apartment).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'adresse' in the 17th century, which derives from the verb 'adresser' (to direct), ultimately from Latin 'directus'.
examples: Wie ist Ihre Adresse? (What is your address?)
Ich habe eine neue Adresse. (I have a new address.)
details.usage: Used for both physical and email addresses (E-Mail-Adresse), and appears in compounds like Adressbuch (address book).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'numerus' (number), entering German via Italian or French in the late Middle Ages.
examples: Was ist deine Telefonnummer? (What is your phone number?)
Die Nummer stimmt nicht. (The number is not correct.)
details.usage: Refers to assigned numbers (phone numbers, house numbers), while 'Zahl' refers to mathematical numbers; appears in compounds like Hausnummer and Kontonummer (account number).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sita' (side, flank), from Proto-Germanic *sidon (side, direction).
examples: Bitte öffnen Sie Seite 10. (Please open page 10.)
Auf der linken Seite ist ein Park. (On the left side there is a park.)
details.usage: Means both 'page' (of a book) and 'side' (of a street or object), and is used in compounds like Webseite (website) and Seitenzahl (page number).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bilidi' (image, likeness), from Proto-Germanic *bilithja, originally meaning 'likeness' or 'form'.
examples: Das Bild an der Wand ist schön. (The picture on the wall is beautiful.)
Ich male ein Bild. (I am painting a picture.)
details.usage: Used for pictures, images, and paintings; appears in compounds like Bildschirm (screen), Bilderbuch (picture book), and the tabloid newspaper 'Bild-Zeitung'.

details.etymology: Derived from 'Zeit' (time) with the suffix '-ung', originally meaning 'news' or 'tidings' in the 17th century.
examples: Ich lese jeden Morgen die Zeitung. (I read the newspaper every morning.)
Steht das in der Zeitung? (Is that in the newspaper?)
details.usage: Well-known German newspapers include Süddeutsche Zeitung, Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung (FAZ), and the tabloid Bild-Zeitung.

details.etymology: Shortened from 'Radioapparat', borrowed from English 'radio' in the 1920s, which derives from Latin 'radius' (ray, beam).
examples: Ich höre Radio. (I listen to the radio.)
Im Radio kommt gute Musik. (There is good music on the radio.)
details.usage: In some regions of Germany the article varies (das/der Radio), but 'das Radio' is standard; used in compounds like Radiosender (radio station).

details.etymology: Compound of 'fern' (far) and 'Seher' (viewer, from 'sehen' = to see), literally meaning 'far-viewer', coined as a German alternative to 'Television'.
examples: Der Fernseher ist neu. (The television is new.)
Abends sehen wir oft Fernseher. (In the evenings we often watch television.)
details.usage: Refers to the TV set itself, while 'Fernsehen' (noun) refers to television as a medium; related compounds include Fernsehprogramm (TV program) and Fernsehsendung (TV broadcast).

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'Internet' in the 1990s, a blend of 'inter' (between, from Latin) and 'network'.
examples: Ich brauche Internet. (I need internet.)
Hast du hier Internet? (Do you have internet here?)
details.usage: Has no plural; used in compounds like Internetseite (website), Internetanschluss (internet connection), and the colloquial phrase 'im Internet surfen' (to surf the internet).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Old French 'pris' (price, value), which comes from Latin 'pretium' (price, reward).
examples: Was ist der Preis? (What is the price?)
Der Preis ist zu hoch. (The price is too high.)
details.usage: Means both 'price' (cost) and 'prize' (award), used in compounds like Nobelpreis (Nobel Prize) and Sonderpreis (special price/sale).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gast' (stranger, guest), from Proto-Germanic *gastiz, cognate with Latin 'hostis' (stranger, enemy) and English 'guest'.
examples: Wir haben heute Gäste. (We have guests today.)
Der Gast bestellt ein Bier. (The guest orders a beer.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Gasthaus (guesthouse/inn), Gastfreundschaft (hospitality), and Gastarbeiter (guest worker, a significant term in German post-war history).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fest' (firm, fixed), as a noun it developed the meaning of a 'fixed/established celebration', from Latin 'festum' (feast).
examples: Wir feiern ein Fest. (We are celebrating a festival.)
Das Fest war sehr schön. (The celebration was very nice.)
details.usage: Used in many compound words for celebrations: Oktoberfest, Weihnachtsfest (Christmas celebration), Volksfest (folk festival), and Sommerfest (summer party).

details.etymology: From the verb 'schenken' (to give, pour) with the prefix 'ge-', originally related to the act of pouring a drink for someone as a gift.
examples: Das Geschenk ist für dich. (The gift is for you.)
Ich suche ein Geschenk für meine Mutter. (I am looking for a gift for my mother.)
details.usage: Central to German gift-giving culture; appears in compounds like Geburtstagsgeschenk (birthday present) and Weihnachtsgeschenk (Christmas present).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Geburt' (birth) and 'Tag' (day), literally meaning 'birth day', from Old High German 'giburt' + 'tag'.
examples: Alles Gute zum Geburtstag! (Happy birthday!)
Mein Geburtstag ist im Mai. (My birthday is in May.)
details.usage: In Germany, it is considered bad luck to wish someone happy birthday early; compounds include Geburtstagskuchen (birthday cake) and Geburtstagsfeier (birthday party).

details.etymology: Feminine form of 'Freund' (friend), from Old High German 'friunt' (friend, lover), with the suffix '-in' for the female form.
examples: Meine Freundin kommt aus München. (My girlfriend comes from Munich.)
Sie ist eine gute Freundin. (She is a good friend.)
details.usage: Can mean either 'female friend' or 'girlfriend' depending on context; using 'eine Freundin' usually means a friend, while 'meine Freundin' often implies a romantic partner.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'nahgibur', compound of 'nah' (near) and 'gibur' (dweller, farmer), literally meaning 'near-dweller'.
examples: Unser Nachbar ist sehr nett. (Our neighbor is very nice.)
Ich kenne meine Nachbarn gut. (I know my neighbors well.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Nachbarschaft (neighborhood), Nachbarland (neighboring country), and the saying 'Liebe deinen Nachbarn' (love thy neighbor).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mennisco', from Proto-Germanic *manniskaz, derived from *mann- (man, human being).
examples: Jeder Mensch ist anders. (Every person is different.)
Mensch, das ist toll! (Wow, that is great!)
details.usage: Used as a general term for 'human being' regardless of gender, and also as an exclamation ('Mensch!') expressing surprise; appears in Menschenrechte (human rights).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'liuti' (people), from Proto-Germanic *liudiz (people, folk), cognate with Russian 'lyudi' (people).
examples: Viele Leute waren da. (Many people were there.)
Die Leute sind freundlich. (The people are friendly.)
details.usage: Used only in plural form as an informal word for 'people'; the singular equivalent would be 'Person' or 'Mensch'.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'herro' (lord, master), the comparative form of 'her' (noble, venerable), from Proto-Germanic *hairaz.
examples: Guten Tag, Herr Müller. (Good day, Mr. Müller.)
Der Herr möchte bezahlen. (The gentleman would like to pay.)
details.usage: Used as the standard formal address for men (like 'Mr.'), and also means 'gentleman' or historically 'lord'; 'Herrentoilette' means men's restroom.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'jungo' (young one), derived from 'jung' (young), from Proto-Germanic *jungaz.
examples: Der Junge spielt Fußball. (The boy plays football.)
Die Jungen und Mädchen lernen zusammen. (The boys and girls learn together.)
details.usage: A masculine noun declined like a weak noun (den Jungen, dem Jungen); in northern Germany 'Junge' is also used colloquially as an exclamation meaning 'boy!' or 'man!'.

details.etymology: Diminutive of 'Magd' (maiden, maid), formed with the suffix '-chen'; from Old High German 'magad' (virgin, young woman).
examples: Das Mädchen liest ein Buch. (The girl reads a book.)
Die Mädchen spielen im Garten. (The girls play in the garden.)
details.usage: Grammatically neuter (das) because all '-chen' diminutives are neuter in German, even though it refers to a female person.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'leben', from Proto-Germanic *libanan (to live, remain), functioning as both a verb and a nominalized noun.
examples: Das Leben ist schön. (Life is beautiful.)
Er lebt ein gutes Leben. (He lives a good life.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like Lebensmittel (groceries, literally 'means of life'), Lebensqualität (quality of life), and Lebenslauf (curriculum vitae).

details.etymology: Derived from 'gesund' (healthy) with the abstract noun suffix '-heit', from Old High German 'gisunt' (healthy, safe).
examples: Gesundheit ist wichtig. (Health is important.)
Gesundheit! (Bless you! — said after someone sneezes)
details.usage: Famously used as an exclamation when someone sneezes, even adopted into English for this purpose; has no plural form as an abstract noun.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hungar', from Proto-Germanic *hungruz, cognate with English 'hunger'.
examples: Ich habe Hunger. (I am hungry.)
Der Hunger ist groß. (The hunger is great.)
details.usage: Typically used without a plural; the expression 'Hunger haben' (to have hunger) is the standard way to say 'to be hungry' in German.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'durst', from Proto-Germanic *thurstuz (thirst, dryness), cognate with English 'thirst'.
examples: Ich habe Durst. (I am thirsty.)
Bei Durst sollte man Wasser trinken. (When thirsty, one should drink water.)
details.usage: Like Hunger, it uses the 'haben' construction ('Durst haben' = to be thirsty); the colloquial expression 'einen über den Durst trinken' means to have one too many drinks.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'angust', from Proto-Germanic *angustu- (tightness, distress), related to Latin 'angustus' (narrow).
examples: Ich habe Angst vor Spinnen. (I am afraid of spiders.)
Du brauchst keine Angst zu haben. (You do not need to be afraid.)
details.usage: Borrowed into English as a loanword meaning existential dread; in German it simply means 'fear' and is used with 'haben' (Angst haben = to be afraid).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'hilfa', from the verb 'helfan' (to help), from Proto-Germanic *helpan.
examples: Hilfe! Ich brauche Hilfe! (Help! I need help!)
Vielen Dank für Ihre Hilfe. (Thank you very much for your help.)
details.usage: Used as an exclamation in emergencies and in compounds like Erste Hilfe (first aid), Haushaltshilfe (household help), and Selbsthilfe (self-help).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'problema', from Greek 'problema' (obstacle, task), from 'proballein' (to throw forward).
examples: Das ist kein Problem. (That is no problem.)
Wir haben ein Problem. (We have a problem.)
details.usage: The phrase 'Kein Problem' is widely used in everyday German; appears in compounds like Problemlösung (problem-solving) and is sometimes colloquially shortened to 'Probs'.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'idée', from Latin 'idea', from Greek 'idea' (form, notion), derived from 'idein' (to see).
examples: Das ist eine gute Idee! (That is a good idea!)
Hast du eine Idee? (Do you have an idea?)
details.usage: Commonly used in everyday conversation; the expression 'keine Idee haben' means to have no clue, and it appears in phrases like 'Idee und Umsetzung' (idea and execution).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'bīspel', a compound of 'bī' (by) and 'spel' (narrative, tale), originally meaning a parable or illustrative story.
examples: Können Sie mir ein Beispiel geben? (Can you give me an example?)
Das ist ein gutes Beispiel für Teamarbeit. (That is a good example of teamwork.)
details.usage: Very common in the phrase 'zum Beispiel' (for example), abbreviated as 'z.B.', which is one of the most frequently used expressions in German.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'pausa' (a halt, stop), which itself came from Greek 'pausis' (cessation).
examples: Wir machen jetzt eine Pause. (We are taking a break now.)
Die Pause dauert fünfzehn Minuten. (The break lasts fifteen minutes.)
details.usage: Used for school breaks (Schulpause), work breaks, and intermissions at theaters or concerts; compound 'Mittagspause' means lunch break.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'anafang', derived from the verb 'anfangen' (to begin), combining 'an' (on) and 'fangen' (to catch).
examples: Am Anfang war es schwer. (In the beginning it was hard.)
Der Anfang des Films ist spannend. (The beginning of the film is exciting.)
details.usage: Often used in the expression 'Anfang Mai' (beginning of May) for approximate dates, and 'aller Anfang ist schwer' is a well-known proverb meaning every beginning is difficult.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'enti', related to Old English 'ende', both from Proto-Germanic '*andijaz' meaning end or boundary.
examples: Das Ende des Buches ist traurig. (The end of the book is sad.)
Am Ende der Straße ist ein Park. (At the end of the street there is a park.)
details.usage: Frequently used in expressions like 'am Ende' (in the end), 'Ende gut, alles gut' (all's well that ends well), and compounds like 'Wochenende' (weekend).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'weg', from Proto-Germanic '*wegaz' meaning way or path, related to Latin 'vehere' (to carry, transport).
examples: Der Weg zum Bahnhof ist nicht weit. (The way to the train station is not far.)
Kannst du mir den Weg zeigen? (Can you show me the way?)
details.usage: Appears in many compounds like 'Gehweg' (sidewalk), 'Radweg' (bike path), and 'Ausweg' (way out); not to be confused with the adverb 'weg' (away).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'brucca', from Proto-Germanic '*brukjō', originally meaning a log or beam laid across water.
examples: Die Brücke über den Fluss ist sehr alt. (The bridge over the river is very old.)
Wir gehen über die Brücke. (We are walking across the bridge.)
details.usage: Used both literally and figuratively to mean a connection between things; 'Brückentag' refers to a day taken off between a holiday and a weekend.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ecka' meaning corner or edge, from Proto-Germanic '*agjō', related to Latin 'acies' (sharp edge).
examples: Der Laden ist um die Ecke. (The shop is around the corner.)
Das Kind steht in der Ecke. (The child is standing in the corner.)
details.usage: Common in phrases like 'um die Ecke' (around the corner) and 'an allen Ecken und Enden' (everywhere); also used in soccer for a corner kick (Eckball).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mitti', from Proto-Germanic '*midjō', cognate with English 'middle' and Latin 'medius'.
examples: Berlin Mitte ist sehr bekannt. (Berlin Mitte is very well known.)
Stell den Tisch in die Mitte des Zimmers. (Put the table in the middle of the room.)
details.usage: Widely used for approximate time references like 'Mitte Juni' (mid-June) or 'Mitte dreißig' (mid-thirties); Berlin-Mitte is a famous district name.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'seifa', borrowed from a Germanic root related to the process of dripping or filtering, possibly connected to early soap-making techniques.
examples: Wasch dir die Hände mit Seife! (Wash your hands with soap!)
Diese Seife riecht gut. (This soap smells good.)
details.usage: Forms compounds like 'Seifenblase' (soap bubble) and 'Seifenoper' (soap opera); historically, German soap-making was an important craft.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Hand' (hand) and 'Tuch' (cloth), literally meaning a cloth for the hands, dating back to Middle High German.
examples: Ich brauche ein sauberes Handtuch. (I need a clean towel.)
Das Handtuch hängt im Bad. (The towel is hanging in the bathroom.)
details.usage: Germans are humorously known for reserving sun loungers with towels on vacation; 'das Handtuch werfen' (to throw in the towel) means to give up.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'douche', which came from Italian 'doccia' (water pipe), ultimately from Latin 'ducere' (to lead).
examples: Ich gehe schnell unter die Dusche. (I'm quickly taking a shower.)
Die Dusche im Hotel ist kaputt. (The shower in the hotel is broken.)
details.usage: The verb 'duschen' (to shower) is derived from it, and 'Duschgel' (shower gel) is a common compound in everyday use.

details.etymology: A compound of 'kühl' (cool) and 'Schrank' (cabinet), literally meaning a cooling cabinet, coined when refrigeration technology became household in the early 20th century.
examples: Die Milch steht im Kühlschrank. (The milk is in the fridge.)
Wir brauchen einen neuen Kühlschrank. (We need a new fridge.)
details.usage: A classic example of German compound nouns; informally shortened to 'Kühlschrank' is already standard, though some dialects use further abbreviations.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'herd' meaning hearth or fireplace, from Proto-Germanic '*herþaz', originally referring to a place for fire in the home.
examples: Der Topf steht auf dem Herd. (The pot is on the stove.)
Bitte schalte den Herd aus. (Please turn off the stove.)
details.usage: In modern German it means stove or cooktop; the old meaning of hearth survives in the proverb 'eigener Herd ist Goldes wert' (one's own hearth is worth gold).

details.etymology: A compound of 'waschen' (to wash) and 'Maschine' (machine), literally a washing machine, formed as the appliance became common in German households.
examples: Die Waschmaschine ist kaputt. (The washing machine is broken.)
Ich muss die Waschmaschine anmachen. (I have to turn on the washing machine.)
details.usage: A typical long German compound word; in apartment buildings, shared laundry rooms ('Waschküche') with communal machines are common in Germany.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kussin', borrowed from Latin 'coxinus' or 'culcitīnum', a diminutive related to cushioning or padding.
examples: Das Kissen ist sehr weich. (The pillow is very soft.)
Leg dein Kissen auf das Sofa. (Put your pillow on the sofa.)
details.usage: Can refer to both a pillow and a cushion; compounds include 'Kopfkissen' (head pillow) and 'Sofakissen' (sofa cushion).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'deccī', related to the verb 'decken' (to cover), from Proto-Germanic '*þakjan'.
examples: Im Winter brauche ich eine warme Decke. (In winter I need a warm blanket.)
Die Decke im Zimmer ist sehr hoch. (The ceiling in the room is very high.)
details.usage: Has two main meanings: blanket and ceiling; the idiom 'mir fällt die Decke auf den Kopf' means to feel cooped up or stir-crazy.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'schranc' meaning barrier or enclosure, related to 'schränken' (to cross, to bar).
examples: Meine Kleider sind im Schrank. (My clothes are in the wardrobe.)
Der Schrank ist aus Holz. (The wardrobe is made of wood.)
details.usage: Forms many compounds: 'Kleiderschrank' (wardrobe), 'Kühlschrank' (fridge), 'Bücherschrank' (bookcase); a very productive word in German.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'regalis' via French, but in German it shifted meaning from something royal to a shelf or rack for storage.
examples: Die Bücher stehen im Regal. (The books are on the shelf.)
Kannst du das Regal aufbauen? (Can you assemble the shelf?)
details.usage: Commonly used for bookshelves and storage units; IKEA's 'Billy' shelf is perhaps the most famous 'Regal' in German households.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'dose', borrowed from Middle Low German, ultimately from Medieval Latin 'dosa' meaning container or box.
examples: Kannst du die Dose öffnen? (Can you open the can?)
In der Dose ist Thunfisch. (There is tuna in the can.)
details.usage: Can mean a tin can or a small container; 'Dosenpfand' refers to Germany's deposit system on cans, and 'Konservendose' is a canned food tin.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'flasca', from Proto-Germanic '*flaskō', possibly related to a plaited or woven covering used around early bottles.
examples: Eine Flasche Wasser, bitte. (A bottle of water, please.)
Die Flasche ist leer. (The bottle is empty.)
details.usage: In colloquial German, 'Flasche' can also mean a loser or incompetent person; 'Flaschenpfand' refers to Germany's bottle deposit return system.

details.etymology: From Middle Low German 'tute' meaning a cone-shaped container or bag, related to the idea of a rolled paper cone.
examples: Brauchen Sie eine Tüte? (Do you need a bag?)
Die Tüte ist aus Papier. (The bag is made of paper.)
details.usage: In northern Germany 'Tüte' is the standard word for a shopping bag, while in southern Germany 'Sackerl' or 'Beutel' may be used instead.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'receptum' (something received), from 'recipere' (to receive), originally a medical term for a doctor's prescription.
examples: Hast du ein Rezept für Kuchen? (Do you have a recipe for cake?)
Der Arzt hat mir ein Rezept gegeben. (The doctor gave me a prescription.)
details.usage: Has two meanings in German: a cooking recipe and a medical prescription; context usually makes the meaning clear.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'restaurant', originally meaning a restorative food or broth, from the verb 'restaurer' (to restore).
examples: Wir gehen heute Abend ins Restaurant. (We are going to a restaurant tonight.)
Das Restaurant ist sehr beliebt. (The restaurant is very popular.)
details.usage: Pronounced with a French-influenced stress on the last syllable in German; 'Gaststätte' is a more traditional German synonym.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'rechnen' (to calculate), from Old High German 'rehhanōn', with the suffix '-ung' forming a noun of action.
examples: Die Rechnung, bitte! (The bill, please!)
Ich habe die Rechnung schon bezahlt. (I have already paid the bill.)
details.usage: Used for both restaurant bills and invoices; in Germany it is common to ask 'Zusammen oder getrennt?' (together or separate) when paying.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'kelner', derived from Latin 'cellenarius' (cellar keeper), originally the person who managed the wine cellar.
examples: Der Kellner bringt das Essen. (The waiter brings the food.)
Wir rufen den Kellner. (We are calling the waiter.)
details.usage: The female form is 'Kellnerin'; the more formal term 'Ober' is also used, and calling out 'Herr Ober!' was traditional but is now considered old-fashioned.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Speise' (food, dish) and 'Karte' (card, map), literally meaning a food card or menu.
examples: Kann ich die Speisekarte haben? (Can I have the menu?)
Auf der Speisekarte gibt es viele Gerichte. (There are many dishes on the menu.)
details.usage: The standard word for a restaurant menu; 'Getränkekarte' is the separate drinks menu that many German restaurants also provide.

details.etymology: From the verb 'berufen' (to call, to appoint), related to the idea of a calling or vocation, from Old High German 'biruofan'.
examples: Was ist Ihr Beruf? (What is your profession?)
Er hat einen interessanten Beruf. (He has an interesting profession.)
details.usage: One of the first questions in German small talk; 'Was sind Sie von Beruf?' is a standard way to ask about someone's occupation.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'bureau' (office, desk), which originally referred to a type of coarse cloth used to cover desks.
examples: Ich bin gerade im Büro. (I am at the office right now.)
Das Büro ist im zweiten Stock. (The office is on the second floor.)
details.usage: Very common in compounds like 'Reisebüro' (travel agency), 'Bürostuhl' (office chair), and 'Großraumbüro' (open-plan office).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'chef' (head, leader), ultimately from Latin 'caput' (head).
examples: Mein Chef ist sehr nett. (My boss is very nice.)
Der Chef hat eine Besprechung. (The boss has a meeting.)
details.usage: In German 'Chef' means boss, not cook (unlike in English/French); the female form is 'Chefin', and 'Chefkoch' specifically means head chef.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'collega' (one chosen alongside another), a compound of 'com-' (together) and 'legare' (to appoint).
examples: Mein Kollege hilft mir oft. (My colleague often helps me.)
Ich gehe mit meinen Kollegen essen. (I am going to eat with my colleagues.)
details.usage: The female form is 'Kollegin'; in German workplaces, 'Kollegen' is also used more broadly to address coworkers in meetings.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'firma' (signature, company name), ultimately from Latin 'firmare' (to confirm or make firm).
examples: Die Firma hat 200 Mitarbeiter. (The company has 200 employees.)
Er arbeitet bei einer großen Firma. (He works at a large company.)
details.usage: A common everyday word for company or firm; synonyms include 'Unternehmen' (enterprise) and 'Betrieb' (business operation).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'terminus' (boundary, limit, end point), which in German shifted to mean a fixed point in time.
examples: Ich habe morgen einen Termin beim Arzt. (I have a doctor's appointment tomorrow.)
Können wir einen Termin machen? (Can we make an appointment?)
details.usage: Germans place high importance on punctuality and appointments; 'Termin' is used for doctor visits, business meetings, and official deadlines alike.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'passeport' and Italian 'passo' (passage), ultimately from Latin 'passus' (step, passage).
examples: Zeigen Sie bitte Ihren Pass. (Please show your passport.)
Mein Pass ist abgelaufen. (My passport has expired.)
details.usage: Refers to a passport ('Reisepass') but also a mountain pass ('Gebirgspass') or a ball pass in sports; context determines the meaning.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'charta' (paper, document), which came from Greek 'khartēs' (sheet of papyrus).
examples: Ich möchte zwei Karten für das Konzert. (I would like two tickets for the concert.)
Hast du eine Karte von Berlin? (Do you have a map of Berlin?)
details.usage: A versatile word meaning card, ticket, or map depending on context; compounds include 'Fahrkarte' (travel ticket), 'Landkarte' (map), and 'Kreditkarte' (credit card).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'posta' (station, relay), from Latin 'posita' (placed), referring to relay stations for mail delivery.
examples: Ist Post für mich da? (Is there mail for me?)
Ich gehe zur Post. (I am going to the post office.)
details.usage: Refers to both mail and the post office; Deutsche Post is Germany's national postal service, and 'Postleitzahl' (PLZ) is the postal code.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'paquet' (package), which is a diminutive of 'pac' (bundle), related to English 'pack'.
examples: Ich habe ein Paket bekommen. (I received a package.)
Das Paket ist sehr schwer. (The package is very heavy.)
details.usage: With the rise of online shopping, 'Paket' and 'Paketzustellung' (package delivery) have become very frequent in everyday German.

details.etymology: A compound of 'nach' (after) and 'richten' (to direct), originally meaning something directed or communicated to someone.
examples: Ich habe eine Nachricht für dich. (I have a message for you.)
Hast du die Nachrichten gesehen? (Did you see the news?)
details.usage: In singular it means a message, but in plural 'Nachrichten' commonly means the news; 'Tagesschau' is Germany's most-watched Nachrichtensendung (news broadcast).

details.etymology: From the verb 'springen' (to jump), from Old High German 'springan', with the typical strong verb noun formation.
examples: Der Sprung ins Wasser war hoch. (The jump into the water was high.)
Das Glas hat einen Sprung. (The glass has a crack.)
details.usage: Means both a jump and a crack; the idiom 'jemandem auf die Sprünge helfen' means to help someone along or give them a hint.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'liod' (song), from Proto-Germanic '*leuþą', related to Gothic 'liuþ' (song, hymn).
examples: Dieses Lied ist sehr schön. (This song is very beautiful.)
Wir singen ein Lied zusammen. (We are singing a song together.)
details.usage: The German 'Lied' has been borrowed into English to refer specifically to German art songs; 'Volkslied' means folk song and 'Kinderlied' means children's song.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stimna', from Proto-Germanic '*stemnō', meaning voice or sound.
examples: Sie hat eine schöne Stimme. (She has a beautiful voice.)
Ich habe meine Stimme verloren. (I have lost my voice.)
details.usage: Means both voice and vote; 'abstimmen' means to vote, and 'Stimme abgeben' means to cast a vote, showing the semantic link between speaking and voting.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'costa' (rib, side, coast), via Middle Low German, referring to the side of a landmass facing the sea.
examples: Die Küste ist wunderschön. (The coast is beautiful.)
Wir fahren an die Küste. (We are driving to the coast.)
details.usage: Germany has coasts on both the North Sea (Nordseeküste) and Baltic Sea (Ostseeküste), both popular vacation destinations.

details.etymology: From Latin 'insula' (island), borrowed into Old High German as 'insela'.
examples: Sylt ist eine bekannte Insel. (Sylt is a well-known island.)
Wir machen Urlaub auf einer Insel. (We are vacationing on an island.)
details.usage: Germany's most famous islands include Sylt, Rügen, and Helgoland; 'Halbinsel' means peninsula (half-island).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'strant', from Proto-Germanic '*strandą', meaning shore or beach.
examples: Die Kinder spielen am Strand. (The children are playing at the beach.)
Der Strand ist sehr sauber. (The beach is very clean.)
details.usage: Germany's North Sea and Baltic beaches feature iconic 'Strandkörbe' (hooded beach chairs), which are a cultural symbol of German seaside holidays.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'classis' (a group, division), originally referring to Roman citizen classes.
examples: Ich bin in Klasse 5. (I am in grade 5.)
Die Klasse hat 25 Schüler. (The class has 25 students.)
details.usage: Means both a school class and a category or quality; colloquially 'klasse!' is used as an adjective meaning great or awesome.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'prüfen' (to test, examine), borrowed from Latin 'probare' (to test, prove), with the noun suffix '-ung'.
examples: Die Prüfung war sehr schwer. (The exam was very difficult.)
Ich muss mich auf die Prüfung vorbereiten. (I have to prepare for the exam.)
details.usage: Used for exams, tests, and inspections; the 'Führerscheinprüfung' (driving license test) is notoriously difficult in Germany.

details.etymology: A compound of 'Blei' (lead) and 'Stift' (pin, stick), literally a lead stick, from the historical belief that graphite was a form of lead.
examples: Hast du einen Bleistift? (Do you have a pencil?)
Schreib bitte mit Bleistift. (Please write in pencil.)
details.usage: Despite being made of graphite, the name preserves the old association with lead; 'Buntstift' (colored pencil) follows the same compound pattern.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tavala', borrowed from Latin 'tabula' (board, plank, tablet).
examples: Der Lehrer schreibt an die Tafel. (The teacher writes on the blackboard.)
Eine Tafel Schokolade, bitte. (A bar of chocolate, please.)
details.usage: Means both a blackboard and a bar (of chocolate); 'die Tafel' also refers to charitable food distribution organizations across Germany.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'regula' (straight stick, rule), from 'regere' (to guide, to rule).
examples: Du musst die Regeln beachten. (You must follow the rules.)
In der Regel trinke ich Kaffee am Morgen. (I usually drink coffee in the morning.)
details.usage: The phrase 'in der Regel' (as a rule, usually) is very common; Germans are often associated with a cultural respect for rules and order.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'üben' (to practice), from Old High German 'uoben', with the noun suffix '-ung'.
examples: Übung macht den Meister. (Practice makes perfect.)
Machen Sie bitte Übung drei. (Please do exercise three.)
details.usage: Used for both practice and exercises in textbooks; 'Übung macht den Meister' is one of the best-known German proverbs.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'textus' (a weaving, texture, context), from 'texere' (to weave), referring to the weaving together of words.
examples: Lesen Sie den Text auf Seite zehn. (Read the text on page ten.)
Der Text ist auf Deutsch. (The text is in German.)
details.usage: Very common in language learning contexts; also used for song lyrics ('Liedtext') and text messages ('Textnachricht').

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'thema', from Greek 'thema' (something laid down, a proposition), from 'tithenai' (to place).
examples: Was ist das Thema heute? (What is the topic today?)
Das Thema ist sehr interessant. (The topic is very interesting.)
details.usage: The irregular plural 'Themen' reflects its Greek-Latin origin; 'kein Thema' (no issue) is a casual way of saying it is not a problem.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'gruppo' (knot, cluster) or French 'groupe', which entered German in the 17th century.
examples: Wir arbeiten in einer Gruppe. (We work in a group.)
Die Gruppe hat zehn Personen. (The group has ten people.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Arbeitsgruppe' (working group), 'Reisegruppe' (travel group), and 'Gruppenarbeit' (group work).

details.etymology: A compound of 'Haus' (house) and 'Tier' (animal), literally meaning a house animal or domestic pet.
examples: Hast du ein Haustier? (Do you have a pet?)
Katzen sind beliebte Haustiere. (Cats are popular pets.)
details.usage: Germany is a very pet-friendly country with approximately 34 million pets; dogs are even allowed on public transport in many cities.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'swīn', from Proto-Germanic '*swīną', cognate with English 'swine' and Latin 'suinus' (of pigs).
examples: Das Schwein ist auf dem Bauernhof. (The pig is on the farm.)
Schwein haben ist ein Ausdruck für Glück. (To have a pig is an expression for luck.)
details.usage: In German culture pigs symbolize luck; 'Schwein haben' means to be lucky, and pig figurines are common New Year's Eve good-luck charms.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'huon', from Proto-Germanic '*hōną', related to Latin 'canere' (to sing), originally referring to the crowing bird.
examples: Das Huhn legt Eier. (The chicken lays eggs.)
Wir haben drei Hühner im Garten. (We have three chickens in the garden.)
details.usage: 'Hähnchen' (a diminutive of 'Hahn', rooster) refers to chicken meat for eating, while 'Huhn' is the live animal; 'Hühnerstall' means chicken coop.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scāf', from Proto-Germanic '*skēpą', the origin of which is uncertain but possibly related to shearing.
examples: Das Schaf steht auf der Wiese. (The sheep is standing on the meadow.)
Die Schafe geben uns Wolle. (The sheep give us wool.)
details.usage: The idiom 'das schwarze Schaf' (the black sheep) is used just like in English to mean the odd one out in a group.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'haso', from Proto-Germanic '*hasô', possibly related to a root meaning gray, referring to the animal's color.
examples: Der Hase läuft über das Feld. (The hare runs across the field.)
Kinder lieben den Osterhasen. (Children love the Easter Bunny.)
details.usage: The 'Osterhase' (Easter Bunny) is a central figure in German Easter traditions; 'mein Hase' is also used as a term of endearment.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'slango', related to the verb 'schlingen' (to wind, twist), describing the snake's winding movement.
examples: Die Schlange ist im Gras. (The snake is in the grass.)
Wir stehen in einer langen Schlange. (We are standing in a long queue.)
details.usage: Has two meanings: snake and queue (line of people); 'Schlange stehen' (to stand in a queue) is a very common everyday expression.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'frosc', from Proto-Germanic '*fruskaz', of uncertain ultimate origin.
examples: Der Frosch sitzt am Teich. (The frog is sitting by the pond.)
Im Märchen wird ein Frosch zum Prinzen. (In the fairy tale a frog turns into a prince.)
details.usage: The fairy tale 'Der Froschkönig' (The Frog King) by the Brothers Grimm is one of Germany's most famous stories.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'chevar', from Proto-Germanic '*kabrô', referring to a gnawing or chewing insect.
examples: Der Käfer krabbelt auf dem Blatt. (The beetle is crawling on the leaf.)
Der VW Käfer ist ein berühmtes Auto. (The VW Beetle is a famous car.)
details.usage: Also the nickname of the Volkswagen Beetle car ('VW Käfer'), one of the most iconic German automobiles; 'Marienkäfer' means ladybug.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'flioga', derived from the verb 'fliogan' (to fly), from Proto-Germanic '*fleugǭ'.
examples: Eine Fliege ist im Zimmer. (There is a fly in the room.)
Er trägt eine Fliege zum Anzug. (He wears a bow tie with his suit.)
details.usage: Means both a housefly and a bow tie; the idiom 'zwei Fliegen mit einer Klappe schlagen' means to kill two birds with one stone.

details.etymology: From Latin 'rosa', borrowed into Old High German, ultimately from Greek 'rhodon' (rose), possibly of Iranian origin.
examples: Er schenkt ihr eine rote Rose. (He gives her a red rose.)
Die Rosen im Garten blühen. (The roses in the garden are blooming.)
details.usage: As in many cultures, red roses symbolize love in Germany; 'Rosenmontag' (Rose Monday) is the highlight of Carnival celebrations.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gemüese', a collective noun related to 'Mus' (mush, puree), originally referring to cooked plant food.
examples: Ich esse gern Gemüse. (I like eating vegetables.)
Das Gemüse ist frisch. (The vegetables are fresh.)
details.usage: A collective uncountable noun in German (no distinct plural form); individual vegetables have their own names like 'Karotte', 'Gurke', etc.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zwibollo', borrowed from Latin 'cepulla' (small onion), a diminutive of 'cepa' (onion).
examples: Schneide bitte die Zwiebel. (Please cut the onion.)
Zwiebeln machen mich weinen. (Onions make me cry.)
details.usage: Essential in German cooking; 'Zwiebelkuchen' (onion cake) is a savory autumn specialty often paired with 'Federweißer' (young wine).

details.etymology: Borrowed from a Slavic language (compare Polish 'ogórek'), which in turn came from Medieval Greek 'angourion' (cucumber).
examples: Ich möchte eine Gurke kaufen. (I would like to buy a cucumber.)
Die Gurke ist sehr frisch. (The cucumber is very fresh.)
details.usage: The 'Spreewaldgurke' (Spreewald pickle) from Brandenburg is a famous German regional specialty and a protected product.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'moraha', related to Proto-Germanic '*murhōn', referring to an edible root vegetable.
examples: Die Möhre ist orange. (The carrot is orange.)
Hasen fressen gern Möhren. (Rabbits like to eat carrots.)
details.usage: Regional variation exists: 'Möhre' is used mainly in northern Germany, while 'Karotte' is more common in the south and in Austria.

details.etymology: Likely from the German dialectal word 'Knödel' (dumpling) or from an early form meaning a small lump of dough.
examples: Ich esse gern Nudeln. (I like eating pasta.)
Die Nudeln sind fertig. (The pasta is ready.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural 'Nudeln' when referring to pasta; 'Spätzle', a Swabian egg noodle dish, is a beloved regional 'Nudel' variety.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'pizza', whose exact origin is debated but may come from Greek 'pitta' (flatbread) or Langobardic 'bizzo' (bite).
examples: Ich bestelle eine Pizza. (I am ordering a pizza.)
Die Pizza schmeckt sehr gut. (The pizza tastes very good.)
details.usage: One of the most popular foods in Germany; both 'Pizzas' and 'Pizzen' are accepted as plural forms, and pizza delivery is widespread.

details.etymology: A diminutive of 'Hahn' (rooster) with the suffix '-chen', literally meaning a small rooster, used to refer to a young chicken for eating.
examples: Ich nehme das Hähnchen mit Reis. (I'll have the chicken with rice.)
Das Hähnchen ist knusprig. (The chicken is crispy.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to chicken as food; regional variants include 'Hendl' (Bavaria) and 'Broiler' (eastern Germany).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Spanish 'chocolate', which came from Nahuatl 'xocolātl' (bitter water), the Aztec name for the cacao drink.
examples: Ich mag Schokolade sehr gern. (I like chocolate very much.)
Möchtest du eine heiße Schokolade? (Would you like a hot chocolate?)
details.usage: Germany is one of the world's largest chocolate consumers; colloquially shortened to 'Schoko' in compounds like 'Schokoriegel' (chocolate bar).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'īs', from Proto-Germanic '*īsą', meaning ice; the food meaning (ice cream) developed later.
examples: Im Sommer esse ich gern Eis. (In summer I like to eat ice cream.)
Vorsicht, das Eis ist glatt! (Careful, the ice is slippery!)
details.usage: Means both ice and ice cream; Italian-style 'Eisdielen' (ice cream parlors) are a beloved tradition in German cities.

details.etymology: A diminutive of 'Brot' (bread) with the suffix '-chen', literally meaning a small bread or bread roll.
examples: Zum Frühstück esse ich Brötchen. (For breakfast I eat bread rolls.)
Ich kaufe frische Brötchen beim Bäcker. (I buy fresh rolls at the bakery.)
details.usage: Called 'Brötchen' in northern Germany but 'Semmel' in Bavaria and Austria, and 'Schrippe' in Berlin — a classic example of German regional vocabulary.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Portuguese 'marmelada' (quince paste), from 'marmelo' (quince), ultimately from Latin 'melimelum' (honey apple).
examples: Ich esse Brötchen mit Marmelade. (I eat bread rolls with jam.)
Die Marmelade ist selbstgemacht. (The jam is homemade.)
details.usage: In everyday German 'Marmelade' means any jam, but EU regulations technically restrict the term to citrus-based preserves; 'Konfitüre' is the official term for other fruit jams.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'honag', from Proto-Germanic '*hunagą', possibly related to a root meaning golden or yellow.
examples: Ich trinke Tee mit Honig. (I drink tea with honey.)
Der Honig kommt vom Imker. (The honey comes from the beekeeper.)
details.usage: Germany is the world's largest honey importer per capita; 'jemandem Honig um den Mund schmieren' means to butter someone up.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'sauce', ultimately from Latin 'salsa' (salted), from 'sal' (salt).
examples: Die Soße schmeckt lecker. (The sauce tastes delicious.)
Möchtest du Soße zum Fleisch? (Would you like sauce with the meat?)
details.usage: Also spelled 'Sauce' in German; 'Bratensoße' (gravy) and 'Tomatensoße' (tomato sauce) are common compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wurst', from Proto-Germanic '*wurstiz', possibly related to a root meaning to turn or twist, describing how sausages are made.
examples: Ich möchte eine Wurst, bitte. (I would like a sausage, please.)
In Deutschland gibt es viele Wurstsorten. (In Germany there are many types of sausage.)
details.usage: Germany has over 1,500 types of sausage; the idiom 'es ist mir Wurst' means I don't care, and 'Currywurst' is an iconic Berlin street food.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'skinko' (leg, shank), from Proto-Germanic '*skinkô', originally referring to the leg of an animal.
examples: Ich esse gern Brot mit Schinken. (I like eating bread with ham.)
Der Schinken ist vom Metzger. (The ham is from the butcher.)
details.usage: 'Schwarzwälder Schinken' (Black Forest ham) is a famous German specialty with protected geographical indication.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'torta' (cake), which came from Late Latin 'torta' (a round bread), possibly from 'tortus' (twisted).
examples: Die Torte sieht wunderschön aus. (The cake looks beautiful.)
Zum Geburtstag gibt es eine Torte. (There is a cake for the birthday.)
details.usage: In German, 'Torte' specifically refers to a layered, decorated cake (often with cream or fruit), while 'Kuchen' is a simpler, everyday cake.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'portio' (share, part), related to 'pars' (part).
examples: Die Portion ist sehr groß. (The portion is very big.)
Ich möchte eine kleine Portion Pommes. (I would like a small portion of fries.)
details.usage: Common in restaurants when ordering; 'eine Portion Glück' (a portion of luck) is a figurative expression, and 'eine gute Portion Humor' means a good deal of humor.

details.etymology: From the verb 'richten' (to direct, arrange), with the prefix 'ge-'; the food meaning refers to something prepared or arranged.
examples: Was ist Ihr Lieblingsgericht? (What is your favorite dish?)
Dieses Gericht ist typisch deutsch. (This dish is typically German.)
details.usage: Has two distinct meanings: a dish/meal and a court of law; 'Hauptgericht' means main course and 'Amtsgericht' means district court.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Erde' (earth) and 'Beere' (berry), referring to the fruit growing close to the ground.
examples: Die Erdbeere ist sehr süß. (The strawberry is very sweet.)
Ich kaufe Erdbeeren auf dem Markt. (I buy strawberries at the market.)
details.usage: Commonly used in compounds like 'Erdbeerkuchen' (strawberry cake) and 'Erdbeermarmelade' (strawberry jam).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'trūba', related to a Germanic root meaning cluster or bunch.
examples: Die Trauben hängen am Weinstock. (The grapes hang on the vine.)
Möchtest du rote oder grüne Trauben? (Would you like red or green grapes?)
details.usage: Often used in compounds like 'Traubensaft' (grape juice) and 'Weintraube' (wine grape).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'citrone', ultimately from Latin 'citrus'.
examples: Ich trinke Wasser mit Zitrone. (I drink water with lemon.)
Die Zitrone ist sehr sauer. (The lemon is very sour.)
details.usage: Frequently found in compounds like 'Zitronensaft' (lemon juice) and 'Zitronenkuchen' (lemon cake).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kirsa', borrowed from Latin 'cerasus' (cherry tree).
examples: Die Kirschen sind reif. (The cherries are ripe.)
Ich esse gern Kirschen im Sommer. (I like to eat cherries in summer.)
details.usage: Common in compounds like 'Kirschkuchen' (cherry cake) and 'Kirschsaft' (cherry juice).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'bira', borrowed from Latin 'pirum' (pear).
examples: Die Birne schmeckt süß. (The pear tastes sweet.)
Kaufst du bitte zwei Birnen? (Can you please buy two pears?)
details.usage: Also used colloquially to mean 'light bulb' (Glühbirne) due to the similar shape.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pfeffar', borrowed from Latin 'piper', ultimately from Sanskrit 'pippali'.
examples: Gib bitte mehr Pfeffer auf das Essen. (Please put more pepper on the food.)
Der Pfeffer ist sehr scharf. (The pepper is very spicy.)
details.usage: Found in compounds like 'Pfeffermühle' (pepper mill) and in the idiom 'wo der Pfeffer wächst' (where the pepper grows, meaning far away).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'melo', related to the verb 'mahlen' (to grind).
examples: Ich brauche Mehl für den Kuchen. (I need flour for the cake.)
Das Mehl ist im Schrank. (The flour is in the cupboard.)
details.usage: A staple in German baking, used in compounds like 'Weizenmehl' (wheat flour) and 'Vollkornmehl' (wholegrain flour).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'oli', borrowed from Latin 'oleum' (olive oil).
examples: Ich brate mit Öl. (I fry with oil.)
Das Öl ist in der Flasche. (The oil is in the bottle.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like 'Olivenöl' (olive oil), 'Sonnenblumenöl' (sunflower oil), and 'Ölfarbe' (oil paint).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ezzih', borrowed from Latin 'acetum' (vinegar).
examples: Ich mache den Salat mit Essig und Öl. (I make the salad with vinegar and oil.)
Der Essig ist sehr sauer. (The vinegar is very sour.)
details.usage: Often paired with 'Öl' in the phrase 'Essig und Öl' for salad dressing, and used in compounds like 'Apfelessig' (apple cider vinegar).

details.etymology: Compound of the prefix 'ab-' (off, away) and 'Fall' (fall), literally meaning something that falls off or away.
examples: Wirf den Abfall in den Mülleimer. (Throw the waste in the trash can.)
Hier liegt viel Abfall auf der Straße. (There is a lot of litter on the street.)
details.usage: Used interchangeably with 'Müll' in everyday speech, and appears in compounds like 'Abfalleimer' (waste bin) and 'Abfalltrennung' (waste separation).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'slāf', related to the verb 'schlafen' (to sleep), from a Germanic root meaning slack or limp.
examples: Ich brauche mehr Schlaf. (I need more sleep.)
Der Schlaf war gut letzte Nacht. (The sleep was good last night.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like 'Schlafzimmer' (bedroom), 'Schlafanzug' (pajamas), and 'Schlaflosigkeit' (insomnia).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'troum', from a Germanic root related to deceiving or deluding.
examples: Ich hatte einen schönen Traum. (I had a beautiful dream.)
Mein Traum ist eine Reise nach Japan. (My dream is a trip to Japan.)
details.usage: Used both literally and figuratively, appearing in compounds like 'Traumhaus' (dream house) and 'Albtraum' (nightmare).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gelücke', originally meaning fate or fortune, possibly related to 'gelingen' (to succeed).
examples: Viel Glück bei der Prüfung! (Good luck on the exam!)
Das Glück war auf unserer Seite. (Luck was on our side.)
details.usage: Means both 'luck' and 'happiness' in German, used in compounds like 'Glückwunsch' (congratulations) and 'Glücksklee' (four-leaf clover).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'spasso' (amusement, fun) in the 16th century.
examples: Das Spiel macht Spaß. (The game is fun.)
Viel Spaß auf der Party! (Have fun at the party!)
details.usage: Very commonly used in the phrase 'Spaß machen' (to be fun) and 'Spaß haben' (to have fun).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'liubī', related to 'lieb' (dear), from a Germanic root meaning desire or love.
examples: Die Liebe ist wichtig im Leben. (Love is important in life.)
Er zeigt seine Liebe mit kleinen Gesten. (He shows his love with small gestures.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like 'Liebesbrief' (love letter) and 'Lieblingsfarbe' (favorite color), and as a form of address: 'Meine Liebe' (my dear).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ruowa', from a Germanic root meaning rest or stillness.
examples: Ich brauche etwas Ruhe. (I need some quiet.)
In der Bibliothek herrscht Ruhe. (There is silence in the library.)
details.usage: Common in expressions like 'Ruhe bitte!' (Quiet please!) and compounds like 'Ruhetag' (rest day, day off for shops/restaurants).

details.etymology: From Italian 'all'arme' (to arms!), originally a military alarm call that came to mean noise in general.
examples: Der Lärm von der Straße ist laut. (The noise from the street is loud.)
Die Kinder machen viel Lärm. (The children make a lot of noise.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Lärmschutz' (noise protection) and 'Lärmbelästigung' (noise nuisance), reflecting Germany's strict noise regulations.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ding', originally meaning a public assembly or legal matter, later shifting to mean thing or object.
examples: Was ist das für ein Ding? (What kind of thing is that?)
Ich muss ein paar Dinge erledigen. (I need to take care of a few things.)
details.usage: Very common in everyday speech, also used colloquially in 'Dingsbums' or 'Dingsda' as a placeholder when you forget a word.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stucki', meaning piece or fragment, from a Germanic root meaning to cut.
examples: Ich möchte ein Stück Kuchen. (I would like a piece of cake.)
Das Theaterstück war toll. (The play was great.)
details.usage: Extremely versatile, used as a counting word ('zwei Stück' = two pieces) and in compounds like 'Frühstück' (breakfast) and 'Meisterstück' (masterpiece).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'sorta' or French 'sorte', ultimately from Latin 'sors' (lot, fate, kind).
examples: Welche Sorte Käse möchtest du? (Which kind of cheese would you like?)
Es gibt viele Sorten Brot in Deutschland. (There are many kinds of bread in Germany.)
details.usage: Commonly used when talking about varieties of food, drinks, or products, often interchangeable with 'Art' (type).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'lista' (strip, list), ultimately from a Germanic root meaning strip or border.
examples: Ich schreibe eine Einkaufsliste. (I am writing a shopping list.)
Dein Name steht auf der Liste. (Your name is on the list.)
details.usage: Frequently used in compounds like 'Einkaufsliste' (shopping list), 'Wunschliste' (wish list), and 'Warteliste' (waiting list).

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'plan' (plan, map), ultimately from Latin 'planum' (flat surface).
examples: Hast du einen Plan für das Wochenende? (Do you have a plan for the weekend?)
Der Plan hat sich geändert. (The plan has changed.)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like 'Stadtplan' (city map), 'Fahrplan' (timetable), and 'Stundenplan' (class schedule).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'zil', meaning boundary, end point, or target.
examples: Mein Ziel ist es, Deutsch zu lernen. (My goal is to learn German.)
Wir haben unser Ziel erreicht. (We have reached our goal.)
details.usage: Means both 'goal' and 'destination', used in compounds like 'Reiseziel' (travel destination) and 'Ziellinie' (finish line).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'kraft', from a Germanic root meaning strength or power.
examples: Er hat viel Kraft. (He has a lot of strength.)
Die Kraft reicht nicht aus. (The strength is not enough.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Arbeitskraft' (workforce), 'Kraftwerk' (power plant), and the legal term 'in Kraft treten' (to come into effect).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stella', related to the verb 'stellen' (to place, to put).
examples: An deiner Stelle würde ich gehen. (In your place, I would go.)
Er sucht eine neue Stelle. (He is looking for a new job.)
details.usage: Has multiple meanings including 'place/spot', 'position/job', and 'digit', appearing in compounds like 'Arbeitsstelle' (workplace) and 'Haltestelle' (bus stop).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'forma' (shape, form, mold).
examples: Die Vase hat eine schöne Form. (The vase has a beautiful shape.)
Ich bin heute nicht in Form. (I am not in shape today.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Backform' (baking mold), 'Lebensform' (way of life), and the expression 'in Form sein' (to be in shape).

details.etymology: Derived from the adjective 'groß' (big, large) with the suffix '-e', from Old High German 'grōz'.
examples: Welche Größe tragen Sie? (What size do you wear?)
Die Größe des Zimmers ist gut. (The size of the room is good.)
details.usage: Commonly used when shopping for clothes or shoes, and in compounds like 'Kleidergröße' (clothing size) and 'Schuhgröße' (shoe size).

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'richten' (to direct, to straighten) with the suffix '-ung'.
examples: In welche Richtung muss ich gehen? (In which direction do I have to go?)
Der Zug fährt in Richtung München. (The train goes in the direction of Munich.)
details.usage: Essential for giving and understanding directions, often used with prepositions: 'in Richtung' (in the direction of).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ampulla', borrowed from Latin 'ampulla' (small bottle), originally referring to an oil lamp.
examples: Die Ampel ist rot. (The traffic light is red.)
Warte an der Ampel! (Wait at the traffic light!)
details.usage: In political context, 'Ampelkoalition' (traffic light coalition) refers to a coalition of SPD (red), FDP (yellow), and Greens.

details.etymology: Shortened from 'Taxameter', borrowed from French 'taximètre', combining Latin 'taxa' (charge) and Greek 'metron' (measure).
examples: Ich nehme ein Taxi zum Flughafen. (I take a taxi to the airport.)
Das Taxi kommt in fünf Minuten. (The taxi is coming in five minutes.)
details.usage: German taxis are typically cream/beige colored and can be hailed or ordered by phone, with 'Taxistand' (taxi stand) being common at stations.

details.etymology: Compound of 'parken' (to park, from English) and 'Platz' (place, square), literally a place for parking.
examples: Ich suche einen Parkplatz. (I am looking for a parking space.)
Der Parkplatz ist voll. (The parking lot is full.)
details.usage: Can mean both a single parking space and an entire parking lot; 'Parkhaus' refers to a multi-story parking garage.

details.etymology: Compound of 'ein-' (in) and 'Gang' (going, passage), literally meaning the way in.
examples: Der Eingang ist dort drüben. (The entrance is over there.)
Bitte benutzen Sie den Haupteingang. (Please use the main entrance.)
details.usage: The opposite of 'Ausgang' (exit), commonly seen on signs at buildings, and used in compounds like 'Haupteingang' (main entrance).

details.etymology: Compound of 'aus-' (out) and 'Gang' (going, passage), literally meaning the way out.
examples: Wo ist der Ausgang? (Where is the exit?)
Der Notausgang ist links. (The emergency exit is on the left.)
details.usage: Also means 'outcome' or 'result' in abstract usage, and appears in compounds like 'Notausgang' (emergency exit) and 'Ausgangspunkt' (starting point).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fart', related to the verb 'fahren' (to drive, to travel).
examples: Die Fahrt dauert zwei Stunden. (The journey takes two hours.)
Gute Fahrt! (Have a good trip!)
details.usage: Used in many compounds like 'Hinfahrt' (outward journey), 'Rückfahrt' (return journey), and 'Fahrkarte' (travel ticket).

details.etymology: Derived from 'sicher' (safe, secure) with the suffix '-heit', from Old High German 'sihur', borrowed from Latin 'securus'.
examples: Sicherheit geht vor. (Safety comes first.)
Ich bin mir nicht sicher, aber zur Sicherheit frage ich. (I am not sure, but I will ask to be safe.)
details.usage: Means both 'safety' and 'security', used in compounds like 'Sicherheitsgurt' (seatbelt) and 'Sicherheitskontrolle' (security check).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'gebūwede', derived from 'bauen' (to build) with the collective prefix 'ge-'.
examples: Das Gebäude ist sehr alt. (The building is very old.)
In diesem Gebäude gibt es viele Büros. (There are many offices in this building.)
details.usage: A formal word for building, used in compounds like 'Wohngebäude' (residential building) and 'Bürogebäude' (office building).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Stock' (stick, story) and 'Werk' (work, structure), originally referring to the framework of a floor level.
examples: Das Büro ist im dritten Stockwerk. (The office is on the third floor.)
Das Haus hat vier Stockwerke. (The house has four floors.)
details.usage: Synonymous with 'Etage' and 'Geschoss'; note that in German counting, 'erstes Stockwerk' is the second floor in American English.

details.etymology: Compound of 'auf' (up) and 'Zug' (pull), literally meaning something that pulls up.
examples: Nehmen wir den Aufzug oder die Treppe? (Shall we take the elevator or the stairs?)
Der Aufzug ist kaputt. (The elevator is broken.)
details.usage: Synonymous with 'Fahrstuhl' and 'Lift'; also has a secondary meaning of a theatrical act or a procession.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'mieta', meaning reward or payment, from a Germanic root related to compensation.
examples: Die Miete ist 500 Euro im Monat. (The rent is 500 euros per month.)
Die Miete muss am Ersten bezahlt werden. (The rent must be paid on the first.)
details.usage: Central to German housing culture where renting is very common; 'Kaltmiete' is rent without utilities, 'Warmmiete' includes them.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'heizen' (to heat) with the suffix '-ung', from Old High German 'heizen'.
examples: Die Heizung ist an. (The heating is on.)
Im Winter brauchen wir die Heizung. (In winter we need the heating.)
details.usage: Refers to both the heating system and the radiator; heating costs ('Heizkosten') are a standard part of German utility bills.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stroum', meaning stream or current, from a Germanic root meaning to flow.
examples: Der Strom ist teuer in Deutschland. (Electricity is expensive in Germany.)
Wir haben keinen Strom. (We have no electricity.)
details.usage: Means both 'electricity' and 'large river/current', used in compounds like 'Stromrechnung' (electricity bill) and 'Stromausfall' (power outage).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'mül', originally meaning dust or crumbled material, related to grinding.
examples: Bringst du bitte den Müll raus? (Can you please take out the trash?)
Der Müll wird montags abgeholt. (The garbage is collected on Mondays.)
details.usage: Germany has strict waste separation: 'Mülltrennung' is a key cultural practice with separate bins for paper, plastic, glass, and organic waste.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pflanza', borrowed from Latin 'planta' (plant, seedling).
examples: Ich gieße die Pflanzen jeden Tag. (I water the plants every day.)
Diese Pflanze braucht viel Sonne. (This plant needs a lot of sun.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Zimmerpflanze' (houseplant), 'Pflanzenwelt' (plant world), and the verb 'pflanzen' (to plant).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'wisa', from a Germanic root meaning meadow or pasture.
examples: Die Kinder spielen auf der Wiese. (The children play on the meadow.)
Die Wiese ist voller Blumen. (The meadow is full of flowers.)
details.usage: Common in place names across German-speaking regions, and in compounds like 'Blumenwiese' (flower meadow) and the famous Munich 'Theresienwiese' (Oktoberfest grounds).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stein', from a common Germanic word for stone or rock.
examples: Pass auf den Stein auf! (Watch out for the stone!)
Das Haus ist aus Stein gebaut. (The house is built of stone.)
details.usage: Extremely common in surnames (Einstein, Goldstein) and compounds like 'Edelstein' (gemstone) and 'Baustein' (building block).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sant', from a common Germanic word for sand.
examples: Die Kinder spielen im Sand. (The children play in the sand.)
Am Strand gibt es viel Sand. (There is a lot of sand on the beach.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Sandkasten' (sandbox), 'Sandstrand' (sandy beach), and the idiom 'den Kopf in den Sand stecken' (to bury one's head in the sand).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rücken' (back) and 'Sack' (bag), literally meaning back-bag; this German word was borrowed into English.
examples: Mein Rucksack ist sehr schwer. (My backpack is very heavy.)
Ich packe den Rucksack für die Wanderung. (I pack the backpack for the hike.)
details.usage: One of the German words adopted into English; essential for hiking culture in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Regen' (rain) and 'Schirm' (shield, screen), literally a rain shield.
examples: Nimm deinen Regenschirm mit! (Take your umbrella with you!)
Ich habe meinen Regenschirm vergessen. (I forgot my umbrella.)
details.usage: Often shortened to just 'Schirm' in everyday speech; 'Sonnenschirm' (parasol) follows the same pattern.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Uhr' (clock) and 'Zeit' (time), literally meaning clock-time.
examples: Wie ist die Uhrzeit? (What is the time?)
Die Uhrzeit stimmt nicht. (The time is not correct.)
details.usage: Specifically refers to the time of day as shown on a clock, as opposed to 'Zeit' which has broader meanings.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'datum' (given), from the phrase 'datum est' (it was given) used on letters.
examples: Welches Datum ist heute? (What is today's date?)
Bitte schreiben Sie das Datum auf das Formular. (Please write the date on the form.)
details.usage: The plural 'Daten' also means 'data' in modern usage; German date format is day.month.year (e.g., 10.06.2026).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'calendarium' (account book), derived from 'calendae' (the first day of the Roman month).
examples: Ich schreibe den Termin in den Kalender. (I write the appointment in the calendar.)
Der Kalender hängt an der Wand. (The calendar hangs on the wall.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Adventskalender' (Advent calendar), a beloved German Christmas tradition.

details.etymology: Short for 'Füllfederhalter' (fountain pen), derived from 'füllen' (to fill) referring to the ink filling mechanism.
examples: Schreibst du mit einem Füller? (Do you write with a fountain pen?)
Der Füller hat keine Tinte mehr. (The fountain pen has no more ink.)
details.usage: German schoolchildren traditionally learn to write with fountain pens ('Füller'), which are still standard in many German schools.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'krīda', borrowed from Latin 'creta' (chalk, named after the island of Crete).
examples: Der Lehrer schreibt mit Kreide an die Tafel. (The teacher writes on the board with chalk.)
Die Kreide ist weiß. (The chalk is white.)
details.usage: Still used in traditional German classrooms, and appears in the idiom 'Kreide fressen' (to eat chalk, meaning to act innocent).

details.etymology: Compound of 'radieren' (to erase, from Latin 'radere' meaning to scrape) and 'Gummi' (rubber).
examples: Hast du einen Radiergummi? (Do you have an eraser?)
Der Radiergummi ist auf dem Tisch. (The eraser is on the table.)
details.usage: A standard school supply in Germany, sometimes shortened to 'Radierer' in informal speech.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'linealis' (relating to a line), from 'linea' (line).
examples: Zeichne eine Linie mit dem Lineal. (Draw a line with the ruler.)
Das Lineal ist 30 Zentimeter lang. (The ruler is 30 centimeters long.)
details.usage: A standard item in German school pencil cases ('Federmäppchen'), used specifically for the straight-edge ruler.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'scāri', related to the verb 'scheren' (to shear, to cut).
examples: Gib mir bitte die Schere. (Please give me the scissors.)
Die Schere ist scharf. (The scissors are sharp.)
details.usage: Unlike in English, 'Schere' is grammatically singular in German; also used figuratively in 'die Schere zwischen Arm und Reich' (the gap between rich and poor).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'pflastar', borrowed from Latin 'emplastrum' (plaster, poultice), from Greek 'emplastron'.
examples: Ich brauche ein Pflaster für die Wunde. (I need a bandage for the wound.)
Das Pflaster auf der Straße ist alt. (The pavement on the street is old.)
details.usage: Has two meanings: adhesive bandage (Band-Aid) and pavement/cobblestone; 'ein teures Pflaster' is an idiom meaning an expensive place to live.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'fiebar', borrowed from Latin 'febris' (fever).
examples: Das Kind hat Fieber. (The child has a fever.)
Mein Fieber ist 38 Grad. (My fever is 38 degrees.)
details.usage: Measured in Celsius in German-speaking countries; 'Fieberthermometer' (fever thermometer) is a common household item.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'erkälten' (to catch cold), from 'kalt' (cold) with the prefix 'er-' and suffix '-ung'.
examples: Ich habe eine Erkältung. (I have a cold.)
Bei einer Erkältung sollte man viel trinken. (When you have a cold, you should drink a lot.)
details.usage: Distinct from 'Grippe' (flu), which is more serious; Germans often treat colds with herbal teas and rest.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'smerzo', from a Germanic root meaning to sting or hurt.
examples: Ich habe Schmerzen im Rücken. (I have pain in my back.)
Der Schmerz geht nicht weg. (The pain doesn't go away.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural ('Schmerzen') for physical pain, and in compounds like 'Kopfschmerzen' (headache) and 'Schmerzmittel' (painkiller).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'medicina' (the healing art, medicine), from 'medicus' (physician).
examples: Sie studiert Medizin. (She studies medicine.)
Nimm deine Medizin! (Take your medicine!)
details.usage: Refers to both the academic field of medicine and medication itself; 'Medizinstudium' (medical studies) is highly competitive in Germany.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'tablette' (small table, tablet), diminutive of 'table', from Latin 'tabula'.
examples: Nimm zwei Tabletten nach dem Essen. (Take two tablets after eating.)
Die Tabletten helfen gegen Kopfschmerzen. (The tablets help against headaches.)
details.usage: Specifically means a pill or tablet of medicine; not to be confused with 'Tablet' which refers to a tablet computer.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Zahn' (tooth) and 'Arzt' (doctor), literally meaning tooth doctor.
examples: Ich gehe morgen zum Zahnarzt. (I am going to the dentist tomorrow.)
Der Zahnarzt sagt, meine Zähne sind gesund. (The dentist says my teeth are healthy.)
details.usage: Dental care is partially covered by public health insurance ('Krankenkasse') in Germany; the female form is 'Zahnärztin'.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Spiel' (play, game) and 'Platz' (place, square), literally a place for playing.
examples: Die Kinder sind auf dem Spielplatz. (The children are at the playground.)
Der Spielplatz hat eine neue Rutsche. (The playground has a new slide.)
details.usage: German playgrounds are abundant and well-maintained; they often have signs indicating age recommendations and rules.

details.etymology: Compound of 'schwimmen' (to swim) and 'Bad' (bath), literally a swimming bath.
examples: Wir gehen am Samstag ins Schwimmbad. (We are going to the swimming pool on Saturday.)
Das Schwimmbad hat im Winter geschlossen. (The swimming pool is closed in winter.)
details.usage: Can refer to indoor ('Hallenbad') or outdoor ('Freibad') pools; many German towns have public swimming facilities.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'bibliotheca', from Greek 'bibliotheke' (book repository), combining 'biblion' (book) and 'theke' (storage).
examples: Ich leihe Bücher aus der Bibliothek. (I borrow books from the library.)
Die Bibliothek ist bis 20 Uhr geöffnet. (The library is open until 8 PM.)
details.usage: German public libraries ('Stadtbibliothek') offer books, media, and often free internet access; distinct from 'Bücherei' which is more informal.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Rat' (council, advice) and 'Haus' (house), literally the council house.
examples: Das Rathaus steht am Marktplatz. (The town hall stands at the market square.)
Wir müssen zum Rathaus gehen. (We have to go to the town hall.)
details.usage: The Rathaus is the center of local government in German cities and towns, often a historically significant building on the main square.

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'centum' (hundred) via English/French, as one hundredth of a euro.
examples: Das kostet nur 50 Cent. (That costs only 50 cents.)
Hast du ein paar Cent? (Do you have a few cents?)
details.usage: Used as the subdivision of the Euro; in German, the plural remains 'Cent' (not 'Cents').

details.etymology: Named after Europe, introduced as the common currency of the eurozone in 1999 (coins and bills from 2002).
examples: Das Buch kostet zehn Euro. (The book costs ten euros.)
Ich habe nur noch fünf Euro. (I only have five euros left.)
details.usage: In German, the plural is 'Euro' (not 'Euros') in standard usage, and prices are read as 'drei Euro fünfzig' (3.50).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Italian 'conto' (account, bill), from Latin 'computus' (calculation).
examples: Ich eröffne ein Konto bei der Bank. (I am opening an account at the bank.)
Das Geld ist auf meinem Konto. (The money is in my account.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Bankkonto' (bank account), 'Girokonto' (checking account), and 'Sparkonto' (savings account).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'tasten' (to touch, to feel), related to the verb 'tasten' meaning to grope or feel.
examples: Drück die Taste! (Press the key!)
Die Taste funktioniert nicht. (The key is not working.)
details.usage: Refers to keys on a keyboard, buttons on devices, or keys on a piano; used in compounds like 'Tastatur' (keyboard).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Bild' (image, picture) and 'Schirm' (screen, shield), literally an image screen.
examples: Der Bildschirm ist zu hell. (The screen is too bright.)
Ich sitze den ganzen Tag vor dem Bildschirm. (I sit in front of the screen all day.)
details.usage: Refers to any display screen (computer monitor, TV); 'Touchscreen' is also used in German for touch-enabled screens.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'drucken' (to print) with the agent suffix '-er', from Old High German 'drucken' (to press).
