examples: Der Drucker hat kein Papier mehr. (The printer has no more paper.)
Ich muss den Drucker reparieren. (I need to repair the printer.)
details.usage: Can mean both a printer (device) and a printer (person who prints); 'Laserdrucker' and 'Tintenstrahldrucker' (inkjet printer) are common types.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'sezzal', related to 'sitzen' (to sit), from Latin 'sessio' (sitting).
examples: Der Sessel ist sehr bequem. (The armchair is very comfortable.)
Opa sitzt in seinem Sessel. (Grandpa sits in his armchair.)
details.usage: Specifically refers to an upholstered armchair, as distinct from 'Stuhl' (chair); 'Ohrensessel' is a wing chair.

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'teppich', borrowed via Old French from Latin 'tapetum', ultimately from Greek 'tapes' (carpet).
examples: Der Teppich im Wohnzimmer ist neu. (The carpet in the living room is new.)
Bitte die Schuhe auf dem Teppich ausziehen. (Please take off shoes on the carpet.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Teppichboden' (wall-to-wall carpet) and the idiom 'etwas unter den Teppich kehren' (to sweep something under the rug).

details.etymology: Compound of 'vor' (in front of) and 'Hang' (from 'hängen', to hang), literally something hanging in front.
examples: Mach bitte die Vorhänge zu. (Please close the curtains.)
Die Vorhänge sind blau. (The curtains are blue.)
details.usage: In theater, 'der Vorhang fällt' (the curtain falls) signals the end of a performance; 'Gardine' is a lighter, more sheer curtain.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ovan', from a Germanic root, possibly related to Greek 'ipnos' (oven).
examples: Der Kuchen ist im Ofen. (The cake is in the oven.)
Im Winter heizen wir mit dem Ofen. (In winter we heat with the stove.)
details.usage: Can mean both an oven for cooking and a heating stove; 'Backofen' specifies a baking oven, and 'Kachelofen' is a traditional tiled stove.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'spülen' (to rinse, to wash), from Old High German 'spuolen'.
examples: Stell die Teller in die Spüle. (Put the plates in the sink.)
Die Spüle ist aus Edelstahl. (The sink is made of stainless steel.)
details.usage: Refers specifically to a kitchen sink; 'Spülmaschine' (dishwasher) and 'Spülmittel' (dish soap) are related compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ketina', borrowed from Latin 'catena' (chain).
examples: Sie trägt eine goldene Kette. (She wears a golden necklace.)
Der Hund ist an einer Kette. (The dog is on a chain.)
details.usage: Means both a chain and a necklace; also used in compounds like 'Fahrradkette' (bicycle chain) and 'Ladenkette' (retail chain).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'ring', from a common Germanic word meaning circle or ring.
examples: Er hat ihr einen Ring geschenkt. (He gave her a ring as a gift.)
Der Ring passt perfekt. (The ring fits perfectly.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Ehering' (wedding ring), 'Boxring' (boxing ring), and 'Ringstraße' (ring road).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'soc', borrowed from Latin 'soccus' (a type of light shoe).
examples: Ich brauche neue Socken. (I need new socks.)
Eine Socke fehlt in der Waschmaschine. (One sock is missing in the washing machine.)
details.usage: Almost always used in the plural 'Socken'; the idiom 'von den Socken sein' means to be astonished.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'gurtil', derived from 'gurt' (belt, girdle), related to the verb 'gürten' (to gird).
examples: Der Gürtel ist zu lang. (The belt is too long.)
Ich brauche einen schwarzen Gürtel. (I need a black belt.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Sicherheitsgürtel' (safety belt) and in the expression 'den Gürtel enger schnallen' (to tighten one's belt, meaning to economize).

details.etymology: From Middle High German 'mütze', borrowed from Medieval Latin 'almutia' (a hooded cape).
examples: Setz deine Mütze auf, es ist kalt! (Put on your cap, it's cold!)
Die Mütze ist aus Wolle. (The cap is made of wool.)
details.usage: A general term for knitted or casual caps and hats, distinct from 'Hut' (brimmed hat); 'Bademütze' is a swimming cap.

details.etymology: Borrowed from French 'châle', which came from Persian 'shāl' (a type of woven fabric).
examples: Im Winter trage ich immer einen Schal. (In winter I always wear a scarf.)
Der Schal ist sehr warm. (The scarf is very warm.)
details.usage: Refers to a warm scarf for cold weather; a lighter decorative scarf is often called 'Tuch' (cloth/shawl).

details.etymology: Compound of 'Hand' (hand) and 'Schuh' (shoe), literally a shoe for the hand.
examples: Zieh deine Handschuhe an! (Put on your gloves!)
Ich habe meine Handschuhe verloren. (I lost my gloves.)
details.usage: A charming compound word that literally means 'hand shoe'; used in compounds like 'Boxhandschuh' (boxing glove) and 'Handschuhfach' (glove compartment).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'stiful', borrowed from Latin 'aestivale' (summer shoe), though boots are now associated with colder weather.
examples: Ich ziehe meine Stiefel an. (I put on my boots.)
Die Stiefel sind aus Leder. (The boots are made of leather.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Gummistiefel' (rubber boots) and 'Winterstiefel' (winter boots); on Nikolaustag (Dec 6), children put boots by the door for gifts.

details.etymology: Borrowed from English 'pullover' (a garment pulled over the head), from the verb 'pull over'.
examples: Zieh einen Pullover an, es ist kalt. (Put on a sweater, it's cold.)
Der Pullover ist aus Baumwolle. (The sweater is made of cotton.)
details.usage: Often shortened to 'Pulli' in informal German; refers to any sweater or jumper pulled over the head.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'kleiden' (to dress, to clothe) with the suffix '-ung', from Old High German 'kleid' (garment).
examples: Welche Kleidung trägst du gern? (What clothing do you like to wear?)
Die Kleidung ist im Schrank. (The clothing is in the wardrobe.)
details.usage: A collective term for clothing in general, used in compounds like 'Kleidungsstück' (piece of clothing) and 'Berufskleidung' (work clothing).

details.etymology: Borrowed from Latin 'natura' (birth, nature), from 'nasci' (to be born).
examples: Ich bin gern in der Natur. (I like to be in nature.)
Die Natur ist wunderschön im Frühling. (Nature is beautiful in spring.)
details.usage: Used in compounds like 'Naturschutz' (nature conservation), 'Naturwissenschaft' (natural science), and 'Naturpark' (nature park).

details.etymology: From Old High German 'habēn' (to hold, to have), referring to a place that holds or shelters ships.
examples: Das Schiff liegt im Hafen. (The ship is in the harbor.)
Der Hafen von Hamburg ist sehr groß. (The port of Hamburg is very large.)
details.usage: Hamburg is Germany's largest port city; 'Flughafen' (airport, literally flight harbor) is one of the most common compounds.

details.etymology: From Old High German 'dorf', from a common Germanic word meaning village or settlement.
examples: Meine Großeltern wohnen in einem kleinen Dorf. (My grandparents live in a small village.)
Das Dorf hat nur 200 Einwohner. (The village has only 200 inhabitants.)
details.usage: Common in place names ending in '-dorf' (like Düsseldorf); the expression 'die Welt ist ein Dorf' means 'it's a small world'.

details.etymology: Compound of 'hoch' (high) and 'Zeit' (time), originally meaning a high or festive time, which narrowed to mean wedding.
examples: Die Hochzeit ist im Juni. (The wedding is in June.)
Wir sind zur Hochzeit eingeladen. (We are invited to the wedding.)
details.usage: German weddings often include both a civil ceremony at the 'Standesamt' (registry office) and an optional church ceremony.

details.etymology: Derived from the verb 'einladen' (to invite) with the suffix '-ung', combining 'ein-' (in) and 'laden' (to load, to invite).
examples: Danke für die Einladung! (Thank you for the invitation!)
Ich habe eine Einladung zur Party bekommen. (I received an invitation to the party.)
details.usage: In German culture, it is polite to respond promptly to an 'Einladung'; if someone invites you to dinner, the host typically pays.

details.etymology: Compound of 'Küche' (kitchen, from Latin 'coquina') and 'Tisch' (table, from Latin 'discus').
examples: Wir essen am Küchentisch. (We eat at the kitchen table.)
Die Zeitung liegt auf dem Küchentisch. (The newspaper is on the kitchen table.)
details.usage: The kitchen table is central to German family life, often serving as the everyday dining and gathering spot, in contrast to the more formal 'Esstisch' (dining table).
